Category: 23: Construction/2379: Other Heavy and Civil Engineering Construction
Name: Processing of reclaimed asphalt pavement; at processing
Description: Reclaimed asphalt pavement processing energy is provided by Wilburn and Goonan (1998) as 16.5 MJ/ton. In the absence of additional data, energy requirements are assumed to be equally divided between electricity and diesel consumption, as assumed by US EPA (2003) for demolished portland cement concrete processing. Energy units were converted into electricity and diesel quantities  assuming 3,412 btu/kWh and  138,690 btu/gallon diesel, as provided by US EIA (2013). This process does not included fugitive dust emissions released during the grinding process or emissions from manufacturing, maintenance, or disposal/dismantling of the grinding equipment. Processing is assumed to occur 20 km from the road demolition site.

Category: 56: Administrative and Support and Waste Management and Remediation Services/5622: Waste Treatment and Disposal
Name: MSW landfilling of land clearing debris; at MSW landfill
Description:
The process models materials and energy inputs from disposal of land clearing debris (LCD) in an MSW landfill environment.  It also includes the environmental burdens associated MSW landfill construction, operation and closure/post-closure care. A significant fraction of methane produced from wood decomposition is captured and combusted to carbon dioxide at MSW landfills with a gas collection and control system (GCCS). The methane and biogenic carbon dioxide emission from landfills with a GCCS would be lower and higher, respectively, than from landfills without GCCS. The US EPA (2012b) estimated that the temporally-averaged gas collection efficiency for lumber and MDF ranges from 76% to 92%.  Using the average nationwide statistics for the percentage of landfills that have GCCS and assuming a 90% average gas collection efficiency as provided for dimensional lumber in US EPA (2012), and assuming a moisture content of 50% (instead of the 10% used for branches in US EPA (2012)) the methane and carbon dioxide emissions from the landfill placement of one kilogram of LCD in an MSW landfill are estimated to be 0.012 kg, and 0.18 kg, respectively. Due to an absence of TCLP data for LCD, leachate emissions from the MSW landfilling of LCD are not included in this dataset.

Cover soil is assumed to represent 10% of the volume of waste material placed at MSW landfills that use soil as daily cover.  EREF (1999) estimated that 85% of landfills in the US use soil as daily cover.  The mass quantity of cover necessary for the disposal of LCD at an MSW landfill was estimated using the bulk density of loader-compacted LCD as described in Angus-Hankin et al (1995), and using the bulk density of soil as described in USDA (2013).

A detailed estimate of environmental burdens related to CDD material transport to the MSW landfill site was performed using waste tonnage records from three operating Ohio MSW landfill sites. These records provided the transport mode and county and state of origin for recieved tonnages. The Ohio site data were used to estimate fraction of waste transported via short-haul (<322 km) and long-haul (>322 km) as well as weighted average distance for each transportation mode. These fractions and distances were used along with the trasnport mode-specific (i.e., truck, rail) waste fractions fractions calculated from Waste Business Journal database to estimate tonne-km for each transportation mode. 

A detailed estimate of heavy equipment utilization and equipment-specific fuel combustion was developed based on equipment fuel consumption presented by EREF (1999) and based on the operational lifespans and detailed equipment classifications provided in the US EPA (2014) Motor Vehicle Emissions (MOVES) model.

Category: 56: Administrative and Support and Waste Management and Remediation Services/5622: Waste Treatment and Disposal
Name: MSW landfilling of carpet padding; at MSW landfill
Description:
The process models materials and energy inputs as well as leachate volumes expected from the disposal of carpet padding in an MSW landfill environment, using the density of carpet padding products reported by CCG (2006).  It also includes the environmental burdens associated MSW landfill construction, operation and closure/post-closure care. The presence of carpet padding in an MSW landfill is assumed to have a negligible contribution to landfill gas generation; therefore, gas emissions are not included in this dataset. Leachate emissions are expected, but no publically available source of leachate characteristic data was obtained. 

Cover soil is assumed to represent 10% of the volume of waste material placed at MSW landfills that use soil as daily cover.  EREF (1999) estimated that 85% of landfills in the US use soil as daily cover.  The mass quantity of cover necessary for the disposal of carpet padding at an MSW landfill was estimated using the bulk density of carpet padding as described in CCG (2006), and using the bulk density of soil as described in USDA (2013).

A detailed estimate of environmental burdens related to CDD material transport to the MSW landfill site was performed using waste tonnage records from three operating Ohio MSW landfill sites. These records provided the transport mode and county and state of origin for received tonnages. The Ohio site data were used to estimate fraction of waste transported via short-haul (<322 km) and long-haul (>322 km) as well as weighted average distance for each transportation mode. These fractions and distances were used along with the transport mode-specific (i.e., truck, rail) waste fractions calculated from Waste Business Journal (2014) database to estimate tonne-km for each transportation mode. 

A detailed estimate of heavy equipment utilization and equipment-specific fuel combustion was developed based on equipment fuel consumption presented by EREF (1999) and based on the operational lifespans and detailed equipment classifications provided in the US EPA (2014) Motor Vehicle Emissions (MOVES) model.

Category: 56: Administrative and Support and Waste Management and Remediation Services/5622: Waste Treatment and Disposal
Name: Unlined CDD landfilling of land clearing debris; at unlined CDD landfill
Description:
The process models materials and energy inputs and emissions from the degradation of land clearing debris (LCD) in an unlined construction and demolition debris (CDD) landfill.  It also includes the environmental burdens associated cover soil placement and with unlined CDD landfill operation, closure and post-closure care.   

The methane and carbon dioxide emissions from the landfill placement of one kilogram of LCD  in an CDD landfill are estimated to be 0.035 kg, and 0.12 kg, respectively. These values were calculated using the carbon content, and cover soil oxidation values provided in US EPA (2012b) for branches, however, the moisture content assumed for dimensional lumber (as presented in US EPA 2012) was adjusted from the 10% to 50%. Leachate emissions are estimated based on SPLP measurements by Jang (2000) and Townsend et al (1999) for untreated wood, but adjusted for moisture content.

Weekly cover soil is assumed to represent 1.43% (1/7 of 10%) of the landfill volume.  The mass quantity of cover soil was estimated using the density of LCD as described in Angus-Hankin et al (1995), and using the bulk density of soil as described in USDA (2013).

An estimate of environmental burdens related to CDD transport to the landfill site was performed using waste tonnage records from six operating landfill sites. These records provided the transport mode and county and state of origin for received tonnages.

An estimate of heavy equipment utilization and equipment-specific fuel combustion was developed based on equipment fuel consumption presented by EREF (1999) and based on the operational lifespans and detailed equipment classifications provided in the US EPA (2014) Motor Vehicle Emissions (MOVES) model.

An estimate of environmental burdens related to CDD transport to the landfill site was performed using waste tonnage records from six operating landfill sites. These records provided the transport mode and county and state of origin for received tonnages.

An estimate of heavy equipment utilization and equipment-specific fuel combustion was developed based on equipment fuel consumption presented by EREF (1999) and based on the operational lifespans and detailed equipment classifications provided in the US EPA (2014) Motor Vehicle Emissions (MOVES) model.

Category: 56: Administrative and Support and Waste Management and Remediation Services/5622: Waste Treatment and Disposal
Name: Crushing of clay brick; at crushing facility
Description:
This process dataset accounts for the emissions and other environmental burdens associated with the operation of a clay brick crushing processing facility per kilogram of clay brick material. It accounts for diesel and heavy equipment utilization; particulate matter (PM) emissions associated with crushing, screening, stockpiling and unpaved road transport; land occupation; electricity consumption; and water consumption (for PM control from crushing, screening, and conveyor transfer points). Diesel consumption and heavy equipment utilization are based on a detailed equipment inventory performed for a confidential concrete processing operation (IWCS 2016) and using equipment manufacture literature and lifetime diesel consumption values reported for various heavy equipment types by US EPA (2014). The impact of mortar attached to the clay bricks is not quantified in this dataset. 

Several data sources included are for quantifying the equipment and energy use of crushing of concrete into concrete aggregate. This is assumed to be a suitable analog for crushing clay brick given that Portland cement concrete and clay brick have a similar compressive strength; concrete ranges from 20 to 40 MPa (Kosmatka et al., (2011) whereas clay bricks have reported strengths of 15 MPa (Demir et al., 2006; Demir 2005; Phonphuak et al., 2011), 22 MPa (Samara et al., 2009), 25 MPa (Mohajerani et al. 2016), 40 MPa (Sutcu and Akkurt 2009), up to 48 MPa (D. Eliche-Quesada 2015). It is assumed that dust generation resulting from the crushing, screening and stockpiling of crushed clay bricks is also similar to that of crushed concrete.

Category: 31-33: Manufacturing/3279: Other Nonmetallic Mineral Product Manufacturing
Name: Processing of asphalt shingles; ground; at processing plant
Description: This process dataset accounts for the emissions and other environmental burdens associated with the operation of an asphalt shingles processing facility per kilogram of processed material. It accounts for diesel and heavy equipment utilization; particulate matter (PM) emissions associated with shingle grinding, stockpiling and unpaved road transport; land occupation; and water consumption (for PM control from grinding). Grinder diesel consumption is based on a review of grinding equipment listed in permit documents from 7 sites across 4 states; the average grinder horsepower from these facilities was used to select a representative piece of grinding equipment and associated diesel consumption rate. Diesel consumption and equipment utilization for mobile heavy equipment are based on a detailed equipment inventory performed for a confidential concrete processing operation (IWCS 2016) and using equipment manufacture literature and lifetime diesel consumption values reported for various heavy equipment types by US EPA (2014).

Category: 56: Administrative and Support and Waste Management and Remediation Services/5622: Waste Treatment and Disposal
Name: Unlined CDD landfilling of vinyl composition tile, VCT; at unlined CDD landfill
Description:
The process models materials and energy inputs and emissions from the placement of VCT in an unlined construction and demolition debris (CDD) landfill, using the density of VCT as calculated from NIST (2007).  It also includes the environmental burdens associated cover soil placement and with unlined CDD landfill operation, closure and post-closure care. While the presence of VCT in an CDD landfill may produce carbon dioxide and liberate calcium as a result of the reaction of the hydronium ion and the limestone found in VCT, carbon dioxide and calcium emissions could not be estimated because of an absence of synthetic precipitation leaching procedure (SPLP) data. Due to an absence of SPLP data (or other leaching studies that estimate expected leachate emissions in an CDD landfill environment), leachate emissions from the CDD landfilling of this material are not included in this dataset.  

Weekly cover soil is assumed to represent 1.43% (1/7 of 10%) of the landfill volume.  The mass quantity of cover soil was estimated using the density of VCT from the information presented in NIST (2007), and using the bulk density of soil as described in USDA (2013).

Category: 56: Administrative and Support and Waste Management and Remediation Services/5622: Waste Treatment and Disposal
Name: Processing of land clearing debris; mulched; at processing facility
Description: This process dataset accounts for the emissions and other environmental burdens associated with the operation of a land clearing debris (LCD) processing facility per kilogram of throughput. It accounts for diesel and heavy equipment utilization, particulate matter (PM) emissions associated with stockpiling and unpaved road transport, electricity consumption, water consumption (for PM control from LCD grinding), and total hydrocarbon and methanol emissions from LCD grinding. Diesel consumption and heavy equipment utilization are based on information reported by Springsteen et al. (2011) for operation of an LCD processing operation to produce a biomass fuel end product.

Category: 56: Administrative and Support and Waste Management and Remediation Services/5622: Waste Treatment and Disposal
Name: Unlined CDD landfilling of fiberglass insulation; at unlined CDD landfill
Description:
The process models gas emissions, leachate emissions, materials and energy inputs, and equipment emissions from the placement of fiberglass insulation in an unlined construction and demolition debris (CDD) landfill, using a calculated density of the average compacted fiberglass insulation product.  It also includes the environmental burdens associated cover soil placement and with unlined CDD landfill operation, closure and post-closure care. 

Due to an absence of SPLP data (or other leaching studies that estimate expected leachate emissions in an CDD landfill environment) specific to fiberglass insulation, studies quantifying the leachate emissions from the individual layers of fiberglass insulation (i.e., fiberglass batt, kraft paper, asphalt binder) were sought. Leached emissions of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) from asphalt shingles were utilized from Azah (2011) and adjusted for the asphalt content of asphalt shingles (i.e., 22% from US EPA (2015)) and the quantity of asphalt binder in kraft-faced fiberglass insulation (calculated as approximately 3%), assuming that the asphalt in shingles is responsible for the leaching of PAHs.

Gas emissions were estimated assuming the decomposition of the kraft paper facing used in faced fiberglass insulation.  The mass of kraft paper facing used in the average discarded US fiberglass insulation product was calculated using 2002 economic census data (USCB 2005) on the total sales value of major categories of fiberglass insulation products.

Weekly cover soil is assumed to represent 1.43% (1/7 of 10%) of the landfill volume.  The mass quantity of cover soil was estimated using the calculated, compacted density of fiberglass insulation with the compression factor calculated in IWCS (2017).

An estimate of environmental burdens related to CDD transport to the landfill site was performed using waste tonnage records from six operating landfill sites. These records provided the transport mode and county and state of origin for received tonnages.

An estimate of heavy equipment utilization and equipment-specific fuel combustion was developed based on equipment fuel consumption presented by EREF (1999) and based on the operational lifespans and detailed equipment classifications provided in the US EPA (2014) Motor Vehicle Emissions (MOVES) model.

Category: 56: Administrative and Support and Waste Management and Remediation Services/5622: Waste Treatment and Disposal
Name: MSW landfilling of carpet; at MSW landfill
Description:
The process models materials and energy inputs as well as leachate volumes expected from the disposal of carpet in an MSW landfill environment, using the density of carpet products reported by CCG (2006).  It also includes the environmental burdens associated MSW landfill construction, operation and closure/post-closure care. The presence of carpet in an MSW landfill is assumed to have a negligible contribution to landfill gas generation; therefore, gas emissions are not included in this dataset. Leachate emissions are included based on leaching studies conducted by Pohland and Rachdawong (1996) and Shoaeioskouei, (2012). 

Cover soil is assumed to represent 10% of the volume of waste material placed at MSW landfills that use soil as daily cover.  EREF (1999) estimated that 85% of landfills in the US use soil as daily cover.  The mass quantity of cover necessary for the disposal of carpet at an MSW landfill was estimated using the bulk density of carpet as described in CCG (2006), and using the bulk density of soil as described in USDA (2013).

A detailed estimate of environmental burdens related to CDD material transport to the MSW landfill site was performed using waste tonnage records from three operating Ohio MSW landfill sites. These records provided the transport mode and county and state of origin for received tonnages. The Ohio site data were used to estimate fraction of waste transported via short-haul (<322 km) and long-haul (>322 km) as well as weighted average distance for each transportation mode. These fractions and distances were used along with the transport mode-specific (i.e., truck, rail) waste fractions calculated from Waste Business Journal (2014) database to estimate tonne-km for each transportation mode. 

A detailed estimate of heavy equipment utilization and equipment-specific fuel combustion was developed based on equipment fuel consumption presented by EREF (1999) and based on the operational lifespans and detailed equipment classifications provided in the US EPA (2014) Motor Vehicle Emissions (MOVES) model.

Category: 56: Administrative and Support and Waste Management and Remediation Services/5622: Waste Treatment and Disposal
Name: Unlined CDD landfilling of copper wire; 12-2; with ground; at unlined CDD landfill
Description:
The process models materials and energy inputs and emissions from the placement of 12-2 (with ground) copper building wire in an unlined construction and demolition debris (CDD) landfill using the density of copper wire as calculated from Cerrowire (2013).  It also includes the environmental burdens associated cover soil placement and with unlined CDD landfill operation, closure and post-closure care. 

Because the specific copper wire modeled by this dataset incorporates a paper sheathing, it is possible that the landfill disposal of this material may contribute to landfill gas emissions. However, because the paper sheathing is located within the outer PVC jacketing and because details were not found on the mass fraction of paper per length of 12-2 copper wire, landfill gas production is not included in this dataset. Due to an absence of SPLP data (or other leaching studies that estimate expected leachate emissions in an CDD landfill environment), leachate emissions from the CDD landfilling of this material are not included in this dataset. However, it should be noted that copper wire PVC insulation and jacketing may contain phthalate-based plasticizers and lead (used as a heat stabilizer) and these components could potentially leach out of the insulation and jacketing in a landfill environment (US EPA, 2008).

Weekly cover soil is assumed to represent 1.43% (1/7 of 10%) of the landfill volume.  The mass quantity of cover soil was estimated using the density of copper wire, calculated from the information presented in Cerrowire (2013), and using the bulk density of soil as described in USDA (2013).

An estimate of environmental burdens related to CDD transport to the landfill site was performed using waste tonnage records from six operating landfill sites. These records provided the transport mode and county and state of origin for received tonnages.

An estimate of heavy equipment utilization and equipment-specific fuel combustion was developed based on equipment fuel consumption presented by EREF (1999) and based on the operational lifespans and detailed equipment classifications provided in the US EPA (2014) Motor Vehicle Emissions (MOVES) model.

Category: 56: Administrative and Support and Waste Management and Remediation Services/5622: Waste Treatment and Disposal
Name: MSW landfilling of alkaline copper quaternary-treated wood; at MSW landfill
Description:
The process models materials and energy inputs as well as gas and leachate emissions from disposal of ACQ-treated wood products in an MSW landfill environment.  It also includes the environmental burdens associated MSW landfill construction, operation and closure/post-closure care.  A significant fraction of methane produced from wood decomposition is captured and combusted to carbon dioxide at MSW landfills with a gas collection and control system (GCCS). The methane and biogenic carbon dioxide emission from landfills with a GCCS would be lower and higher, respectively, than from landfills without GCCS. The US EPA (2012) estimated that the temporally-averaged gas collection efficiency for lumber and MDF ranges from 76% to 92%.  Using the average nationwide statistics for the percentage of landfills that have GCCS and assuming a 90% average gas collection efficiency as provided for dimensional lumber in US EPA (2012b), the methane and carbon dioxide emissions from the landfill placement of one kilogram of wood  in an MSW landfill are estimated to be 0.022 kg, and 0.33 kg, respectively. Over the 40-year gas collection period simulated by this dataset, wood treatment chemicals were assumed to not have an impact on total gas generation from this treated wood product. Leachate emissions are estimated based on TCLP measurements by Dubey et al. (2010).

Cover soil is assumed to represent 10% of the volume of waste material placed at MSW landfills that use soil as daily cover.  EREF (1999) estimated that 85% of landfills in the US use soil as daily cover.  The mass quantity of cover necessary for the disposal of wood products at an MSW landfill was estimated using the bulk density of treated wood products as described in CCG (2006), and using the bulk density of soil as described in USDA (2013).

A detailed estimate of environmental burdens related to CDD material transport to the MSW landfill site was performed using waste tonnage records from three operating Ohio MSW landfill sites. These records provided the transport mode and county and state of origin for received tonnages. The Ohio site data were used to estimate fraction of waste transported via short-haul (<322 km) and long-haul (>322 km) as well as weighted average distance for each transportation mode. These fractions and distances were used along with the transport mode-specific (i.e., truck, rail) waste fractions  calculated from Waste Business Journal database to estimate tonne-km for each transportation mode. 

A detailed estimate of heavy equipment utilization and equipment-specific fuel combustion was developed based on equipment fuel consumption presented by EREF (1999) and based on the operational lifespans and detailed equipment classifications provided in the US EPA (2014) Motor Vehicle Emissions (MOVES) model.

These fractions and distances were used along with the transport mode-specific (i.e., truck, rail) waste fractions calculated from Waste Business Journal (2014) database to estimate tonne-km for each transportation mode. 

A detailed estimate of heavy equipment utilization and equipment-specific fuel combustion was developed based on equipment fuel consumption presented by EREF (1999) and based on the operational lifespans and detailed equipment classifications provided in the US EPA (2014) Motor Vehicle Emissions (MOVES) model.

Category: 56: Administrative and Support and Waste Management and Remediation Services/5622: Waste Treatment and Disposal
Name: Unlined CDD landfilling of untreated wood; at unlined CDD landfill
Description:
The process models materials and energy inputs and emissions from the degradation of untreated wood in an unlined construction and demolition debris (CDD) landfill.  It also includes the environmental burdens associated cover soil placement and with unlined CDD landfill operation, closure and post-closure care.   

As estimated using the carbon content, moisture content, and cover soil oxidation values provided in US EPA (2012b), the methane and carbon dioxide emissions from the landfill placement of one kilogram of wood  in an CDD landfill are estimated to be 0.064 kg, and 0.21 kg, respectively. Leachate emissions are estimated based on SPLP measurements by Jang (2000) and Townsend et al (1999).

Weekly cover soil is assumed to represent 1.43% (1/7 of 10%) of the landfill volume.  The mass quantity of cover soil was estimated using the density of untreated wood as described in CCG (2006), and using the bulk density of soil as described in USDA (2013).

An estimate of environmental burdens related to CDD transport to the landfill site was performed using waste tonnage records from six operating landfill sites. These records provided the transport mode and county and state of origin for received tonnages.

An estimate of heavy equipment utilization and equipment-specific fuel combustion was developed based on equipment fuel consumption presented by EREF (1999) and based on the operational lifespans and detailed equipment classifications provided in the US EPA (2014) Motor Vehicle Emissions (MOVES) model.

Category: 56: Administrative and Support and Waste Management and Remediation Services/5622: Waste Treatment and Disposal
Name: MSW landfilling of gypsum drywall; at MSW landfill
Description:
The process models materials and energy inputs from disposal of gypsum drywall in an MSW landfill environment.  It also includes the environmental burdens associated MSW landfill construction, operation and closure/post-closure care. A significant fraction of methane produced from wood decomposition is captured and combusted to carbon dioxide at MSW landfills with a gas collection and control system (GCCS). The methane and biogenic carbon dioxide emission from landfills with a GCCS would be lower and higher, respectively, than from landfills without GCCS. The US EPA (2012b) estimated that the temporally-averaged gas collection efficiency for lumber and MDF ranges from 76% to 92%.  Using the average nationwide statistics for the percentage of landfills that have GCCS and assuming a 90% average gas collection efficiency as provided for dimensional lumber in US EPA (2012), and assuming a moisture content of 50% (instead of the 10% used for branches in US EPA (2012)) the methane and carbon dioxide emissions from the landfill placement of one kilogram of LCD in an MSW landfill are estimated to be 0.012 kg, and 0.18 kg, respectively. Due to an absence of TCLP data for LCD, leachate emissions from the MSW landfilling of LCD are not included in this dataset.

Cover soil is assumed to represent 10% of the volume of waste material placed at MSW landfills that use soil as daily cover.  EREF (1999) estimated that 85% of landfills in the US use soil as daily cover.  The mass quantity of cover necessary for the disposal of LCD at an MSW landfill was estimated using the bulk density of loader-compacted LCD as described in Angus-Hankin et al (1995), and using the bulk density of soil as described in USDA (2013).

A detailed estimate of environmental burdens related to CDD material transport to the MSW landfill site was performed using waste tonnage records from three operating Ohio MSW landfill sites. These records provided the transport mode and county and state of origin for received tonnages. The Ohio site data were used to estimate fraction of waste transported via short-haul (<322 km) and long-haul (>322 km) as well as weighted average distance for each transportation mode. These fractions and distances were used along with the transport mode-specific (i.e., truck, rail) waste fractions  calculated from Waste Business Journal database to estimate tonne-km for each transportation mode. 

A detailed estimate of heavy equipment utilization and equipment-specific fuel combustion was developed based on equipment fuel consumption presented by EREF (1999) and based on the operational lifespans and detailed equipment classifications provided in the US EPA (2014) Motor Vehicle Emissions (MOVES) model.

Category: 56: Administrative and Support and Waste Management and Remediation Services/5622: Waste Treatment and Disposal
Name: Unlined CDD landfilling of clay brick; at unlined CDD landfill
Description:
The process models materials and energy inputs and emissions from the placement of clay bricks in an unlined construction and demolition debris (CDD) landfill.  It also includes the environmental burdens associated cover soil placement and with unlined CDD landfill operation, closure and post-closure care. Leachate emissions are estimated based on leaching with SPLP solution (Kadir et al., 2017; Kadir et al., 2016; Gupta et al., 2017). Some of the mass of clay brick in this dataset may also include attached mortar, but no specific impacts are included for mortar, and no leaching data specific to mortar has been included in this dataset. 

Weekly cover soil is assumed to represent 1.43% (1/7 of 10%) of the landfill volume.  The mass quantity of cover soil was estimated using the density of clay bricks found in Cavalline and Weggel (2012), and using the bulk density of soil as described in USDA (2013).

An estimate of environmental burdens related to CDD transport to the landfill site was performed using waste tonnage records from six operating landfill sites. These records provided the transport mode and county and state of origin for received tonnages.

An estimate of heavy equipment utilization and equipment-specific fuel combustion was developed based on equipment fuel consumption presented by EREF (1999) and based on the operational lifespans and detailed equipment classifications provided in the US EPA (2014) Motor Vehicle Emissions (MOVES) model.

Category: 56: Administrative and Support and Waste Management and Remediation Services/5622: Waste Treatment and Disposal
Name: MSW landfilling of copper wire; 12-2; with ground; at MSW landfill
Description:
This process models materials and energy inputs as well as leachate volumes expected from the disposal of 12-2 (with ground) copper building wire in an MSW landfill environment using the density of copper wire, as calculated from Cerrowire (2013).  It also includes the environmental burdens associated with MSW landfill construction, operation and closure/post-closure care. 

Because the specific copper wire modeled by this dataset incorporates a paper sheathing, it is possible that the landfill disposal of this material may contribute to landfill gas emissions. However, because the paper sheathing is located within the outer PVC jacketing and because details were not found on the mass fraction of paper per length of 12-2 copper wire, landfill gas production is not included in this dataset. Leachate mass-flow emissions from the landfill disposal of copper wire are not included in this dataset due to an absence of toxicity characteristic leaching procedure (TCLP) data. However, it should be noted that copper wire PVC insulation and jacketing may contain phthalate-based plasticizers and lead (used as a heat stabilizer) and these components could potentially leach out of the insulation and jacketing in a landfill environment (US EPA, 2008).

Cover soil is assumed to represent 10% of the volume of waste material placed at MSW landfills that use soil as daily cover.  EREF (1999) estimated that 85% of landfills in the US use soil as daily cover.  The mass quantity of cover necessary for the disposal of copper wire in an MSW landfill was estimated using the density of 12-2 copper wire as calculated from Cerrowire (2013), and using the bulk density of soil as described in USDA (2013).

A detailed estimate of environmental burdens related to CDD material transport to the MSW landfill site was performed using waste tonnage records from three operating Ohio MSW landfill sites. These records provided the transport mode and county and state of origin for received tonnages. The Ohio site data were used to estimate fraction of waste transported via short-haul (<322 km) and long-haul (>322 km) as well as weighted average distance for each transportation mode. Cover soil is assumed to represent 10% of the volume of waste material placed at MSW landfills that use soil as daily cover.  EREF (1999) estimated that 85% of landfills in the US use soil as daily cover.  The mass quantity of cover necessary for the disposal of clay brick at an MSW landfill was estimated using the bulk density of clay brick as described in CCG (2006), and using the bulk density of soil as described in USDA (2013).

A detailed estimate of environmental burdens related to CDD material transport to the MSW landfill site was performed using waste tonnage records from three operating Ohio MSW landfill sites. These records provided the transport mode and county and state of origin for received tonnages. The Ohio site data were used to estimate fraction of waste transported via short-haul (<322 km) and long-haul (>322 km) as well as weighted average distance for each transportation mode. These fractions and distances were used along with the transport mode-specific (i.e., truck, rail) waste fractions calculated from Waste Business Journal (2014) database to estimate tonne-km for each transportation mode. 

A detailed estimate of heavy equipment utilization and equipment-specific fuel combustion was developed based on equipment fuel consumption presented by EREF (1999) and based on the operational lifespans and detailed equipment classifications provided in the US EPA (2014) Motor Vehicle Emissions (MOVES) model.

Name: Benzene production, production mix, at plant, technology mix, 100% active substance
Description:
TYPE OF DATASET
Each chemical compound is provided in aggregated (LCI results) and level-1 disaggregated (unit process, single operation) form. For more information, see the report (ecoinvent Association, 2020, Data on the Production of Chemicals created for the EU Product Environmental Footprint (PEF) transition phase implementation, www.ecoinvent.org, ecoinvent Association, Zurich, Switzerland). This dataset represents the LCI results.

PROCESS DESCRIPTION
This dataset represents the production of 1 kg of benzene. Benzene (C6H6), the simplest aromatic compound, is a colourless and flammable liquid at room temperature. Benzene is a component of gasoline. It is used as a chemical feedstock primarily for the production of ethylbenzene, cumene, and cyclohexane (Folkins 2000).

This dataset is based on a study published by PlasticsEurope & CEFIC/APPE and conducted by the Institut für Energie- und Umweltforschung Heidelberg (IFEU). For more information please see the IFEU Eco-Profile (IFEU 2013).
The activity starts when the raw materials enter the process.
This activity ends with the production of benzene.

References
ecoinvent (2017) Data on the Production of Chemicals created for the EU Product Environmental Footprint (PEF) pilot phase implementation, www.ecoinvent.org, ecoinvent Association, Zürich, Switzerland
Gendorf (2016) Umwelterklärung 2015, Werk Gendorf Industriepark, www.gendorf.de
Folkins, H. O. 2000. Benzene. Ullmann's Encyclopedia of Industrial Chemistry.
IFEU 2013. Benzene, Toluene, and Xylenes (Aromatics, BTX). PlasticsEurope, February 2013.

Water content of the reference product: 0.0 kg

Biogenic carbon content of the reference product: 0.0 kg

DATA QUALITY ASSESSMENT
The data quality ratings for the datasets were determined as the average of the 5 individual ratings for Technological Representativeness, Geographical Representativeness, Time-related representativeness, Precision/uncertainty, and implementation of the End of Life Formula. The final scores for these 5 descriptors were determined by the independent, external reviewer after a discussion with the internal reviewers. The basis for this determination was generally a contribution analysis of the material and energy inputs as well as direct resource uses and emissions. This process was required by the tender. 
The contribution analysis is based on the most important flows in the dataset, defined in the tender specifications as “the unit processes contributing cumulatively to at least to 80% of the total environmental impact based on characterised and normalised results”. In addition to unit processes, direct emissions also qualified as input exchanges for this approach. For the normalization, the normalisation factors “EC-JRC Global (2010 or 2013), per person” available at http://eplca.jrc.ec.europa.eu/?page_id=140 were used. For each parameter, the DQR scores were chosen to best reflect the conditions and quality of the amount value, the appropriateness of the chosen exchange for the specific needs of the system under analysis, and the quality of the foreground and background data for aggregated inputs of exchanges from the technosphere, i.e. not direct emissions or resource uses.

ENERGY AND TRANSPORT INFORMATION SOURCE
Energy and transport was used in both the foreground and background of this dataset.  When building the dataset, energy and transport demands were supplied directly by datasets provided by Sphera.  The background for every other input from technosphere uses a modified version of the ecoinvent database, created specifically for the PEF.  In this version, every instance of energy and transport supply, anywhere in the database, was replaced by a dataset from Sphera.  This ensures that every demand for energy and transport, in the foreground and in the background, is supplied by a Sphera dataset.

NOT INCLUDED EXCHANGES
All known elementary exchanges are included.

Category: POD|LCA EOL Waste treatments:/1. All Materials EOL
Name: (POD|LCA) Unlined CDD landfilling of concrete; at unlined CDD landfill
Description:
Ethan Ellingboe 6/2/2025: Removed all transportation inputs except waste transport at local unpaved road (assumed transport around landfill site). Note process includes PVC pipe as part of landfill construction and fuel consumption + electricity for landfill operation.

The process models materials and energy inputs and emissions from the placement of concrete in an unlined construction and demolition debris (CDD) landfill.  It also includes the environmental burdens associated cover soil placement and with unlined CDD landfill operation, closure and post-closure care. Leachate emissions are estimated based on SPLP measurements by Jang (2000).

Weekly cover soil is assumed to represent 1.43% (1/7 of 10%) of the landfill volume.  The mass quantity of cover soil was estimated using the density of concrete as described in CCG (2006), and using the bulk density of soil as described in USDA (2013).

An estimate of environmental burdens related to CDD transport to the landfill site was performed using waste tonnage records from six operating landfill sites. These records provided the transport mode and county and state of origin for received tonnages.

An estimate of heavy equipment utilization and equipment-specific fuel combustion was developed based on equipment fuel consumption presented by EREF (1999) and based on the operational lifespans and detailed equipment classifications provided in the US EPA (2014) Motor Vehicle Emissions (MOVES) model.

Category: POD|LCA EOL Waste treatments:/1. All Materials EOL
Name: (POD|LCA) Unlined CDD landfilling of copper wire; 12-2; with ground; at unlined CDD landfill
Description:
Ethan Ellingboe 6/2/2025: Removed all transportation inputs except waste transport at local unpaved road (assumed transport around landfill site). Note process includes PVC pipe as part of landfill construction and fuel consumption + electricity for landfill operation.

The process models materials and energy inputs and emissions from the placement of 12-2 (with ground) copper building wire in an unlined construction and demolition debris (CDD) landfill using the density of copper wire as calculated from Cerrowire (2013).  It also includes the environmental burdens associated cover soil placement and with unlined CDD landfill operation, closure and post-closure care. 

Because the specific copper wire modeled by this dataset incorporates a paper sheathing, it is possible that the landfill disposal of this material may contribute to landfill gas emissions. However, because the paper sheathing is located within the outer PVC jacketing and because details were not found on the mass fraction of paper per length of 12-2 copper wire, landfill gas production is not included in this dataset. Due to an absence of SPLP data (or other leaching studies that estimate expected leachate emissions in an CDD landfill environment), leachate emissions from the CDD landfilling of this material are not included in this dataset. However, it should be noted that copper wire PVC insulation and jacketing may contain phthalate-based plasticizers and lead (used as a heat stabilizer) and these components could potentially leach out of the insulation and jacketing in a landfill environment (US EPA, 2008).

Weekly cover soil is assumed to represent 1.43% (1/7 of 10%) of the landfill volume.  The mass quantity of cover soil was estimated using the density of copper wire, calculated from the information presented in Cerrowire (2013), and using the bulk density of soil as described in USDA (2013).

An estimate of environmental burdens related to CDD transport to the landfill site was performed using waste tonnage records from six operating landfill sites. These records provided the transport mode and county and state of origin for received tonnages.

An estimate of heavy equipment utilization and equipment-specific fuel combustion was developed based on equipment fuel consumption presented by EREF (1999) and based on the operational lifespans and detailed equipment classifications provided in the US EPA (2014) Motor Vehicle Emissions (MOVES) model.

Category: POD|LCA EOL Waste treatments:/1. All Materials EOL
Name: (POD|LCA) MSW landfilling of inert material (clay brick); at MSW landfill
Description:
The process models materials and energy inputs as well as leachate volumes expected from the disposal of clay bricks in an MSW landfill environment, using the average, minimum and maximum density values reported in six studies (Mohajerani et al., 2016; D. Eliche-Quesada, 2015; Phonphuak et al., 2011; Demir, 2006; Demir et al., 2005; Sutcu and Akkurt, 2009).  It also includes the environmental burdens associated MSW landfill construction, operation and closure/post-closure care. The presence of clay bricks in an MSW landfill is assumed to have a negligible contribution to landfill gas generation; therefore, gas emissions are not included in this dataset. Leachate emissions are included based on leaching studies conducted by D. Eliche-Quesada (2015); Ukwatta and Mohjerani, (2017); Mohajerani et al., (2016); Kadir and Mohajerani (2012); Kadir and Mohajerani (2008); Kadir et al., (2016); Gupta et al., (2017); Samara et al., (2016); Lafhaz et al., (2008); and Karius and Hammer (2001). 

Cover soil is assumed to represent 10% of the volume of waste material placed at MSW landfills that use soil as daily cover.  EREF (1999) estimated that 85% of landfills in the US use soil as daily cover.  The mass quantity of cover necessary for the disposal of clay brick at an MSW landfill was estimated using the bulk density of clay brick as described in CCG (2006), and using the bulk density of soil as described in USDA (2013).

A detailed estimate of environmental burdens related to CDD material transport to the MSW landfill site was performed using waste tonnage records from three operating Ohio MSW landfill sites. These records provided the transport mode and county and state of origin for received tonnages. The Ohio site data were used to estimate fraction of waste transported via short-haul (<322 km) and long-haul (>322 km) as well as weighted average distance for each transportation mode. These fractions and distances were used along with the transport mode-specific (i.e., truck, rail) waste fractions calculated from Waste Business Journal (2014) database to estimate tonne-km for each transportation mode. 

A detailed estimate of heavy equipment utilization and equipment-specific fuel combustion was developed based on equipment fuel consumption presented by EREF (1999) and based on the operational lifespans and detailed equipment classifications provided in the US EPA (2014) Motor Vehicle Emissions (MOVES) model.

Category: POD|LCA EOL Waste treatments:/1. All Materials EOL
Name: (POD|LCA) Combustion of land clearing debris; at air curtain incineration
Description: This process simulates the onsite combustion of land clearing debris (LCD) in an air curtain incinerator. In both air curtain and open burning scenarios, equipment is needed to arrange and load the LCD into piles in preparation for burning. This process uses high speed air provided by blowers to contain some emissions from combustion of LCD. Emissions from this process include combustion products, and wood ash. Inputs include equipment and fuel combustion required to operate equipment, as well as diesel used to initiate the reaction

Category: POD|LCA EOL Waste treatments:/1. All Materials EOL
Name: (POD|LCA) Incineration of rubber (proxy - polyvinyl chloride, PVC); at MSW waste-to-energy facility
Description:
EE: This process is a proxy for incineration of rubber, and is identical to the process "Incineration of polyvinyl chloride, PVC; at MSW waste-to-energy facility", with the input transport flow removed. 

A gate-to-gate process modeling incineration of PVC resin at a U.S. municipal solid waste waste-to-energy facility. Based on US EPA (2015), an incineration temperature of 750 degrees Celsius is assumed.

Category: POD|LCA EOL Material Processing:/1. All Materials EOL
Name: (POD|LCA) Recovery of construction and demolition debris; segregated; at mixed CDD processing facility
Description:
This process represents proxy impacts for burdens from scrap preparation facility operation for processing of 1 kg of unspecified construction and demolition scrap. Ethan Ellingboe 6/4/25: Removed transportation of waste to facility, and used USLCI or other available providers, no USEEIO providers (for use as proxy for scrap separation facility construction and process-specific burdens).

This gate-to-gate process dataset simulates the inputs and outputs necessary for the operation of a mixed construction and demolition debris (CDD) material recovery facility (MRF). The process is an agglomeration of data from a nationwide survey of CDD MRFs conducted by the Construction and Demolition Recycling Association (CDRA) and includes detailed infrastructure and equipment inventories from an in-depth study of 2 large-scale mixed CDD MRFs located in the southeastern US. Sixteen respondents to the CDRA (2015) provided information on mixed CDD MRF throughput, diversion rates, facility size (i.e., acreage), electricity consumption, and fuel consumption.

Name: coal transport - Truck
Description: The cradle-to-gate inventory for the transportation of coal aggregated to basin, mine type, and coal type groups and also for the various modes of coal transportation. This inventory includes impacts from fuel combustion for the various modes of coal transportation to power plants - train, truck, tug and barge, ocean vessel, lake vessel, and conveyer. Category: 21: Mining, Quarrying, and Oil and Gas Extraction/2121: Coal Mining
Name: coal transport - Barge
Description: The cradle-to-gate inventory for the transportation of coal aggregated to basin, mine type, and coal type groups and also for the various modes of coal transportation. This inventory includes impacts from fuel combustion for the various modes of coal transportation to power plants - train, truck, tug and barge, ocean vessel, lake vessel, and conveyer. The aggregated inventory is created by using the transportation distances for each mode of transportation for each power plant reporting coal receipts in EIA for the year 2016. These inventories are then added to the power plant as upstream impacts similar to upstream coal. Details on the coal modeling can be found in the NETL Coal Baseline published in 2023: https://doi.org/10.2172/2370100. This process was created with ElectricityLCI (https://github.com/USEPA/ElectricityLCI) version 2.0.0 using the ELCI_2022 configuration.

Category: 22: Utilities/2211: Electric Power Generation, Transmission and Distribution/BIOMASS
Name: Electricity - BIOMASS - Duke Energy Progress East
Description:
Electricity from BIOMASS produced at generating facilities in the Duke Energy Progress East region. This process was created with ElectricityLCI (https://github.com/USEPA/ElectricityLCI) version 2.0.0 using the ELCI_2022 configuration.
This process represents the operational emissions from power plants categorized as biomass by the electricitylci aggregated to the stated region (e.g. balancing authority area: California ISO). These include primary fuels of: black liquor (BLQ), wood/wood waste solids (WDS), wood/wood waste liquids (WDL), biogenic municipal solid waste (MSB), landfill gas (LFG), agricultural byproducts (AB), other biomass liquids (OBL), sludge waste (SLW), other biomass gas (OBG), and other biomass solids (OBS). Currently there are no fuel profiles for these fuel types in part because of the wide array and likely unique characteristics of the fuel used in each plant. Ideally, the fuel inventories would include the uptake of CO2 (biogenic CO2) that would offset the generally higher emissions of these plants, particular for solid fuels. The emissions contained in this process are the reported emissions of all of the facilities in the stated region/fuel type per MWh. The boundary of these emissions are within the boundaries of the electricity generating facilities. This process was created with ElectricityLCI (https://github.com/USEPA/ElectricityLCI) version 2.0.0 using the ELCI_2022 configuration.

Category: 22: Utilities/2211: Electric Power Generation, Transmission and Distribution/BIOMASS
Name: Electricity - BIOMASS - Idaho Power Company
Description:
Electricity from BIOMASS produced at generating facilities in the Idaho Power Company region. This process was created with ElectricityLCI (https://github.com/USEPA/ElectricityLCI) version 2.0.0 using the ELCI_2022 configuration.
This process represents the operational emissions from power plants categorized as biomass by the electricitylci aggregated to the stated region (e.g. balancing authority area: California ISO). These include primary fuels of: black liquor (BLQ), wood/wood waste solids (WDS), wood/wood waste liquids (WDL), biogenic municipal solid waste (MSB), landfill gas (LFG), agricultural byproducts (AB), other biomass liquids (OBL), sludge waste (SLW), other biomass gas (OBG), and other biomass solids (OBS). Currently there are no fuel profiles for these fuel types in part because of the wide array and likely unique characteristics of the fuel used in each plant. Ideally, the fuel inventories would include the uptake of CO2 (biogenic CO2) that would offset the generally higher emissions of these plants, particular for solid fuels. The emissions contained in this process are the reported emissions of all of the facilities in the stated region/fuel type per MWh. The boundary of these emissions are within the boundaries of the electricity generating facilities. This process was created with ElectricityLCI (https://github.com/USEPA/ElectricityLCI) version 2.0.0 using the ELCI_2022 configuration.

Category: 22: Utilities/2211: Electric Power Generation, Transmission and Distribution/BIOMASS
Name: Electricity - BIOMASS - JEA
Description:
Electricity from BIOMASS produced at generating facilities in the JEA region. This process was created with ElectricityLCI (https://github.com/USEPA/ElectricityLCI) version 2.0.0 using the ELCI_2022 configuration.
This process represents the operational emissions from power plants categorized as biomass by the electricitylci aggregated to the stated region (e.g. balancing authority area: California ISO). These include primary fuels of: black liquor (BLQ), wood/wood waste solids (WDS), wood/wood waste liquids (WDL), biogenic municipal solid waste (MSB), landfill gas (LFG), agricultural byproducts (AB), other biomass liquids (OBL), sludge waste (SLW), other biomass gas (OBG), and other biomass solids (OBS). Currently there are no fuel profiles for these fuel types in part because of the wide array and likely unique characteristics of the fuel used in each plant. Ideally, the fuel inventories would include the uptake of CO2 (biogenic CO2) that would offset the generally higher emissions of these plants, particular for solid fuels. The emissions contained in this process are the reported emissions of all of the facilities in the stated region/fuel type per MWh. The boundary of these emissions are within the boundaries of the electricity generating facilities. This process was created with ElectricityLCI (https://github.com/USEPA/ElectricityLCI) version 2.0.0 using the ELCI_2022 configuration.

This process represents the operational emissions from hydro-electric power plants aggregated to the stated region (e.g. balancing authority area: California ISO).The emissions contained in this process are mostly calculated emissions from measured GHG emissions from reservoirs associated with hydro power plants. Emissions are then allocated according to share of economic use of the reservoir. The boundary of these emissions are within the boundaries of the electricity generating facilities and the associated reservoir. See Data Treatment section for more details. This process was created with ElectricityLCI (https://github.com/USEPA/ElectricityLCI) version 2.0.0 using the ELCI_2022 configuration.

Category: 22: Utilities/2211: Electric Power Generation, Transmission and Distribution/GEOTHERMAL
Name: Electricity - GEOTHERMAL - Bonneville Power Administration
Description:
Electricity from GEOTHERMAL produced at generating facilities in the Bonneville Power Administration region. This process was created with ElectricityLCI (https://github.com/USEPA/ElectricityLCI) version 2.0.0 using the ELCI_2022 configuration.
This process represents the cradle-to-gate inventory for electricity production from US geothermal power plants. Power plant construction and operations are included in the boundaries. Power plant emissions include the release of non-condensible gases in the geofluid product at the plant. Profiles were developed for different states based on the available data. Geothermal power plants are based on a variety of technologies. All technologies are captured within the state-level profiles. This inventory is an extension of that described in http://www.netl.doe.gov/energy-analysis/details?id=591. This process was created with ElectricityLCI (https://github.com/USEPA/ElectricityLCI) version 2.0.0 using the ELCI_2022 configuration.

Category: 22: Utilities/2211: Electric Power Generation, Transmission and Distribution/GEOTHERMAL
Name: Electricity - GEOTHERMAL - California Independent System Operator
Description:
Electricity from GEOTHERMAL produced at generating facilities in the California Independent System Operator region. This process was created with ElectricityLCI (https://github.com/USEPA/ElectricityLCI) version 2.0.0 using the ELCI_2022 configuration.
This process represents the cradle-to-gate inventory for electricity production from US geothermal power plants. Power plant construction and operations are included in the boundaries. Power plant emissions include the release of non-condensible gases in the geofluid product at the plant. Profiles were developed for different states based on the available data. Geothermal power plants are based on a variety of technologies. All technologies are captured within the state-level profiles. This inventory is an extension of that described in http://www.netl.doe.gov/energy-analysis/details?id=591. This process was created with ElectricityLCI (https://github.com/USEPA/ElectricityLCI) version 2.0.0 using the ELCI_2022 configuration.

Category: 22: Utilities/2211: Electric Power Generation, Transmission and Distribution/GEOTHERMAL
Name: Electricity - GEOTHERMAL - Nevada Power Company
Description:
Electricity from GEOTHERMAL produced at generating facilities in the Nevada Power Company region. This process was created with ElectricityLCI (https://github.com/USEPA/ElectricityLCI) version 2.0.0 using the ELCI_2022 configuration.
This process represents the cradle-to-gate inventory for electricity production from US geothermal power plants. Power plant construction and operations are included in the boundaries. Power plant emissions include the release of non-condensible gases in the geofluid product at the plant. Profiles were developed for different states based on the available data. This inventory is an extension of that described in http://www.netl.doe.gov/energy-analysis/details?id=591. Name: Electricity - HYDRO - ISO New England
Description:
Electricity from HYDRO produced at generating facilities in the ISO New England region. This process was created with ElectricityLCI (https://github.com/USEPA/ElectricityLCI) version 2.0.0 using the ELCI_2022 configuration.
This process represents the operational emissions from hydro-electric power plants aggregated to the stated region (e.g. balancing authority area: California ISO).The emissions contained in this process are mostly calculated emissions from measured GHG emissions from reservoirs associated with hydro power plants. Emissions are then allocated according to share of economic use of the reservoir. The boundary of these emissions are within the boundaries of the electricity generating facilities and the associated reservoir. See Data Treatment section for more details. Electricity from GAS produced at generating facilities in the City of Tallahassee region. This process was created with ElectricityLCI (https://github.com/USEPA/ElectricityLCI) version 2.0.0 using the ELCI_2022 configuration.
This is an electricity generation process that represents the stated fuel type (e.g. coal, gas) aggregated to the stated region (e.g. balancing authority area: California ISO). The emissions contained in this process are the reported emissions of all of the facilities in the stated region/fuel type per MWh. The boundary of these emissions are within the boundaries of the electricity generating facilities. For the mixed plant category, the emissions can be from any facility within the region that does not match the threshold set in the model configuration file. This process was created with ElectricityLCI (https://github.com/USEPA/ElectricityLCI) version 2.0.0 using the ELCI_2022 configuration.

Category: 22: Utilities/2211: Electric Power Generation, Transmission and Distribution/HYDRO
Name: Electricity - HYDRO - Duke Energy Progress East
Description:
Electricity from HYDRO produced at generating facilities in the Duke Energy Progress East region. This process was created with ElectricityLCI (https://github.com/USEPA/ElectricityLCI) version 2.0.0 using the ELCI_2022 configuration.
This process represents the operational emissions from hydro-electric power plants aggregated to the stated region (e.g. balancing authority area: California ISO).The emissions contained in this process are mostly calculated emissions from measured GHG emissions from reservoirs associated with hydro power plants. Emissions are then allocated according to share of economic use of the reservoir. The boundary of these emissions are within the boundaries of the electricity generating facilities and the associated reservoir. See Data Treatment section for more details. This process was created with ElectricityLCI (https://github.com/USEPA/ElectricityLCI) version 2.0.0 using the ELCI_2022 configuration.

Category: 22: Utilities/2211: Electric Power Generation, Transmission and Distribution/GEOTHERMAL
Name: Electricity - GEOTHERMAL - Public Service Company of New Mexico
Location: Public Service Company of New Mexico
Description:
Electricity from GEOTHERMAL produced at generating facilities in the Public Service Company of New Mexico region. This process was created with ElectricityLCI (https://github.com/USEPA/ElectricityLCI) version 2.0.0 using the ELCI_2022 configuration.
This process represents the cradle-to-gate inventory for electricity production from US geothermal power plants. Power plant construction and operations are included in the boundaries. Power plant emissions include the release of non-condensible gases in the geofluid product at the plant. Profiles were developed for different states based on the available data. Geothermal power plants are based on a variety of technologies. All technologies are captured within the state-level profiles. This inventory is an extension of that described in http://www.netl.doe.gov/energy-analysis/details?id=591. This process was created with ElectricityLCI (https://github.com/USEPA/ElectricityLCI) version 2.0.0 using the ELCI_2022 configuration.

Electricity from MIXED produced at generating facilities in the South Carolina Public Service Authority region. This process was created with ElectricityLCI (https://github.com/USEPA/ElectricityLCI) version 2.0.0 using the ELCI_2022 configuration.
This is an electricity generation process that represents the stated fuel type (e.g. coal, gas) aggregated to the stated region (e.g. balancing authority area: California ISO). The emissions contained in this process are the reported emissions of all of the facilities in the stated region/fuel type per MWh. The boundary of these emissions are within the boundaries of the electricity generating facilities. For the mixed plant category, the emissions can be from any facility within the region that does not match the threshold set in the model configuration file. This process was created with ElectricityLCI (https://github.com/USEPA/ElectricityLCI) version 2.0.0 using the ELCI_2022 configuration.

Category: 22: Utilities/2211: Electric Power Generation, Transmission and Distribution/HYDRO
Name: Electricity - HYDRO - Public Service Company of Colorado
Description:
Electricity from HYDRO produced at generating facilities in the Public Service Company of Colorado region. This process was created with ElectricityLCI (https://github.com/USEPA/ElectricityLCI) version 2.0.0 using the ELCI_2022 configuration.
This process represents the operational emissions from hydro-electric power plants aggregated to the stated region (e.g. balancing authority area: California ISO).The emissions contained in this process are mostly calculated emissions from measured GHG emissions from reservoirs associated with hydro power plants. Emissions are then allocated according to share of economic use of the reservoir. The boundary of these emissions are within the boundaries of the electricity generating facilities and the associated reservoir. See Data Treatment section for more details. This process was created with ElectricityLCI (https://github.com/USEPA/ElectricityLCI) version 2.0.0 using the ELCI_2022 configuration.

Electricity from OIL produced at generating facilities in the New York Independent System Operator region. This process was created with ElectricityLCI (https://github.com/USEPA/ElectricityLCI) version 2.0.0 using the ELCI_2022 configuration.
This is an electricity generation process that represents the stated fuel type (e.g. coal, gas) aggregated to the stated region (e.g. balancing authority area: California ISO). The emissions contained in this process are the reported emissions of all of the facilities in the stated region/fuel type per MWh. The boundary of these emissions are within the boundaries of the electricity generating facilities. For the mixed plant category, the emissions can be from any facility within the region that does not match the threshold set in the model configuration file. This process was created with ElectricityLCI (https://github.com/USEPA/ElectricityLCI) version 2.0.0 using the ELCI_2022 configuration.

Category: 22: Utilities/2211: Electric Power Generation, Transmission and Distribution/HYDRO
Name: Electricity - HYDRO - Tennessee Valley Authority
Description:
Electricity from HYDRO produced at generating facilities in the Tennessee Valley Authority region. This process was created with ElectricityLCI (https://github.com/USEPA/ElectricityLCI) version 2.0.0 using the ELCI_2022 configuration.
This process represents the operational emissions from hydro-electric power plants aggregated to the stated region (e.g. balancing authority area: California ISO).The emissions contained in this process are mostly calculated emissions from measured GHG emissions from reservoirs associated with hydro power plants. Emissions are then allocated according to share of economic use of the reservoir. The boundary of these emissions are within the boundaries of the electricity generating facilities and the associated reservoir. See Data Treatment section for more details. This process was created with ElectricityLCI (https://github.com/USEPA/ElectricityLCI) version 2.0.0 using the ELCI_2022 configuration.

Category: 22: Utilities/2211: Electric Power Generation, Transmission and Distribution/WIND
Name: Electricity - WIND - California Independent System Operator
Description:
Electricity from WIND produced at generating facilities in the California Independent System Operator region. This process was created with ElectricityLCI (https://github.com/USEPA/ElectricityLCI) version 2.0.0 using the ELCI_2022 configuration.
This process represents the cradle-to-gate inventory for electricity production from US wind farms. Inventories were developed for each wind farm in the 2016 or 2020 EIA 860 data, as specified in the model configuration, using a custom life cycle model. Boundaries include wind turbine construction and minor operating emissions from fuel combustion, maintenance, etc. The inventory for the wind farm is allocated over an assumed 30 year life. The aggregation performed in the electricity LCI code will then divide those inventories by the generation in the selected year to provide results on the basis of MWh. This process was created with ElectricityLCI (https://github.com/USEPA/ElectricityLCI) version 2.0.0 using the ELCI_2022 configuration.

Category: 22: Utilities/2211: Electric Power Generation, Transmission and Distribution/SOLAR
Name: Electricity - SOLAR - Southeastern Power Administration
Description:
Electricity from SOLAR produced at generating facilities in the Southeastern Power Administration region. This process was created with ElectricityLCI (https://github.com/USEPA/ElectricityLCI) version 2.0.0 using the ELCI_2022 configuration.
Solar inventories are provided in two parts - construction and from operations. Together they represent the cradle-to-gate inventories for electricity production from US solar photovoltaic power plants. Inventories were developed for each solar PV plant in the 2016 or 2020 EIA 860 data, as specified in the model configuration, using a custom life cycle model. Boundaries include plant construction and minor operating emissions from fuel combustion, etc. The total inventory for the plant construction is allocated over an assumed 30 year life. The resulting yearly construction emissions are divided by the generation for the specified year to provide inventories per MWh (e.g., kg CO2/MWh). This process was created with ElectricityLCI (https://github.com/USEPA/ElectricityLCI) version 2.0.0 using the ELCI_2022 configuration.

Inventories were developed for each solar PV plant in the 2016 or 2020 EIA 860 data, as specified in the model configuration, using a custom life cycle model. Boundaries include plant construction and minor operating emissions from fuel combustion, etc. The total inventory for the plant construction is allocated over an assumed 30 year life. The resulting yearly construction emissions are divided by the generation for the specified year to provide inventories per MWh (e.g., kg CO2/MWh). This process was created with ElectricityLCI (https://github.com/USEPA/ElectricityLCI) version 2.0.0 using the ELCI_2022 configuration.

Electricity from WIND produced at generating facilities in the PacifiCorp East region. This process was created with ElectricityLCI (https://github.com/USEPA/ElectricityLCI) version 2.0.0 using the ELCI_2022 configuration.
This process represents the cradle-to-gate inventory for electricity production from US wind farms. Inventories were developed for each wind farm in the 2016 or 2020 EIA 860 data, as specified in the model configuration, using a custom life cycle model. Boundaries include wind turbine construction and minor operating emissions from fuel combustion, maintenance, etc. The inventory for the wind farm is allocated over an assumed 30 year life. The aggregation performed in the electricity LCI code will then divide those inventories by the generation in the selected year to provide results on the basis of MWh. This process was created with ElectricityLCI (https://github.com/USEPA/ElectricityLCI) version 2.0.0 using the ELCI_2022 configuration.

Category: 22: Utilities/2211: Electric Power Generation, Transmission and Distribution/SOLAR
Name: Electricity - SOLAR - Southwestern Power Administration
Description:
Electricity from SOLAR produced at generating facilities in the Southwestern Power Administration region. This process was created with ElectricityLCI (https://github.com/USEPA/ElectricityLCI) version 2.0.0 using the ELCI_2022 configuration.
Solar inventories are provided in two parts - construction and from operations. Together they represent the cradle-to-gate inventories for electricity production from US solar photovoltaic power plants. Inventories were developed for each solar PV plant in the 2016 or 2020 EIA 860 data, as specified in the model configuration, using a custom life cycle model. Boundaries include plant construction and minor operating emissions from fuel combustion, etc. The total inventory for the plant construction is allocated over an assumed 30 year life. The resulting yearly construction emissions are divided by the generation for the specified year to provide inventories per MWh (e.g., kg CO2/MWh). This process was created with ElectricityLCI (https://github.com/USEPA/ElectricityLCI) version 2.0.0 using the ELCI_2022 configuration.

Category: 22: Utilities/2211: Electric Power Generation, Transmission and Distribution/OTHF
Name: Electricity - OTHF - Western Area Power Administration - Rocky Mountain Region
Location: Western Area Power Administration - Rocky Mountain Region
Description:
Electricity from OTHF produced at generating facilities in the Western Area Power Administration - Rocky Mountain Region region. This process was created with ElectricityLCI (https://github.com/USEPA/ElectricityLCI) version 2.0.0 using the ELCI_2022 configuration.
This is an electricity generation process that represents the stated fuel type (e.g. coal, gas) aggregated to the stated region (e.g. balancing authority area: California ISO). The emissions contained in this process are the reported emissions of all of the facilities in the stated region/fuel type per MWh. The boundary of these emissions are within the boundaries of the electricity generating facilities. For the mixed plant category, the emissions can be from any facility within the region that does not match the threshold set in the model configuration file. This process was created with ElectricityLCI (https://github.com/USEPA/ElectricityLCI) version 2.0.0 using the ELCI_2022 configuration.

Category: 22: Utilities/2211: Electric Power Generation, Transmission and Distribution/SOLAR
Name: Electricity - SOLAR - Southern Company Services, Inc. - Trans
Description:
Electricity from SOLAR produced at generating facilities in the Southern Company Services, Inc. - Trans region. This process was created with ElectricityLCI (https://github.com/USEPA/ElectricityLCI) version 2.0.0 using the ELCI_2022 configuration.
Solar inventories are provided in two parts - construction and from operations. Together they represent the cradle-to-gate inventories for electricity production from US solar photovoltaic power plants. Inventories were developed for each solar PV plant in the 2016 or 2020 EIA 860 data, as specified in the model configuration, using a custom life cycle model. Boundaries include plant construction and minor operating emissions from fuel combustion, etc. The total inventory for the plant construction is allocated over an assumed 30 year life. The resulting yearly construction emissions are divided by the generation for the specified year to provide inventories per MWh (e.g., kg CO2/MWh). This process was created with ElectricityLCI (https://github.com/USEPA/ElectricityLCI) version 2.0.0 using the ELCI_2022 configuration.

Name: natural gas extraction and processing - Permian
Description:
The cradle-to-gate inventory for production of gas aggregated to basin. The NETL natural gas life cycle model includes parameters to generate inventories for natural gas extraction based on basin and geology which determines the gas extraction type (e.g., Appalachian Shale using hydraulic fracturing). 2016 natural gas production then informs the amount of each type of technology/region that form the mix in the regions. These can be further aggregated to a US average. More details are in the natural gas upstream report at the link below

Category: 23: Construction/2371: Utility System Construction
Name: power plant construction - ngcc_const - Bonneville Power Administration
Description: The cradle-to-gate inventory for the construction of the fossil fuel generators (coal, gas, and oil). The inventories represent the construction of either natural gas combined cycle or coal power plants using an economic input output life cycle inventory model (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jclepro.2017.04.150) and the construction costs for these plants as detailed by NETL techno-economics analysis (https://www.netl.doe.gov/energy-analysis/details?id=729). These type different types of power plants are then assigned to the various power plants according to the plant they most resemble. The inventories are then scaled according to power plant capacity relative to the source power plants and the allocated over an assumed 30 year life. The single year of impacts are then divided by the generation of the year selected in the model configuration. This process was created with ElectricityLCI (https://github.com/USEPA/ElectricityLCI) version 2.0.0 using the ELCI_2022 configuration.

Category: 22: Utilities/2212: Natural Gas Distribution
Name: natural gas extraction and processing - Piceance
Description:
The cradle-to-gate inventory for production of gas aggregated to basin. The NETL natural gas life cycle model includes parameters to generate inventories for natural gas extraction based on basin and geology which determines the gas extraction type (e.g., Appalachian Shale using hydraulic fracturing). 2016 natural gas production then informs the amount of each type of technology/region that form the mix in the regions. These can be further aggregated to a US average. More details are in the natural gas upstream report at the link below
https://www.netl.doe.gov/energy-analysis/details?id=3198 This process was created with ElectricityLCI (https://github.com/USEPA/ElectricityLCI) version 2.0.0 using the ELCI_2022 configuration.

Category: 23: Construction/2371: Utility System Construction
Name: power plant construction - ngcc_const - California Independent System Operator
Description: The cradle-to-gate inventory for the construction of the fossil fuel generators (coal, gas, and oil). The inventories represent the construction of either natural gas combined cycle or coal power plants using an economic input output life cycle inventory model (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jclepro.2017.04.150) and the construction costs for these plants as detailed by NETL techno-economics analysis (https://www.netl.doe.gov/energy-analysis/details?id=729). These type different types of power plants are then assigned to the various power plants according to the plant they most resemble. The inventories are then scaled according to power plant capacity relative to the source power plants and the allocated over an assumed 30 year life. The single year of impacts are then divided by the generation of the year selected in the model configuration. This process was created with ElectricityLCI (https://github.com/USEPA/ElectricityLCI) version 2.0.0 using the ELCI_2022 configuration.

Category: 23: Construction/2371: Utility System Construction
Name: power plant construction - wind_const - California Independent System Operator
Description: Wind inventories are provided in two parts - construction and from operations. Together they represent the cradle-to-gate inventories for electricity production from US wind turbine power plants. Inventories were developed for each wind farm in the 2016 or 2020 EIA 860 data, as specified in the model configuration, using a custom life cycle model. Boundaries include wind turbine construction and minor operating emissions from fuel combustion, maintenance, etc. The inventory for the wind farm is allocated over an assumed 30 year life. The aggregation performed in the electricity LCI code will then divide those inventories by the generation in the selected year to provide results on the basis of MWh. This process was created with ElectricityLCI (https://github.com/USEPA/ElectricityLCI) version 2.0.0 using the ELCI_2022 configuration.
