The inventories represent the construction of either natural gas combined cycle or coal power plants using an economic input output life cycle inventory model (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jclepro.2017.04.150) and the construction costs for these plants as detailed by NETL techno-economics analysis (https://www.netl.doe.gov/energy-analysis/details?id=729). These type different types of power plants are then assigned to the various power plants according to the plant they most resemble. The inventories are then scaled according to power plant capacity relative to the source power plants and the allocated over an assumed 30 year life. The single year of impacts are then divided by the generation of the year selected in the model configuration. This process was created with ElectricityLCI (https://github.com/USEPA/ElectricityLCI) version 2.0.0 using the ELCI_2022 configuration.

Category: 23: Construction/2371: Utility System Construction
Name: power plant construction - wind_const - NaturEner Wind Watch, LLC
Description: Wind inventories are provided in two parts - construction and from operations. Together they represent the cradle-to-gate inventories for electricity production from US wind turbine power plants. Inventories were developed for each wind farm in the 2016 or 2020 EIA 860 data, as specified in the model configuration, using a custom life cycle model. Boundaries include wind turbine construction and minor operating emissions from fuel combustion, maintenance, etc. The inventory for the wind farm is allocated over an assumed 30 year life. The aggregation performed in the electricity LCI code will then divide those inventories by the generation in the selected year to provide results on the basis of MWh. This process was created with ElectricityLCI (https://github.com/USEPA/ElectricityLCI) version 2.0.0 using the ELCI_2022 configuration.

The aggregation performed in the electricity LCI code will then divide those inventories by the generation in the selected year to provide results on the basis of MWh. This process was created with ElectricityLCI (https://github.com/USEPA/ElectricityLCI) version 2.0.0 using the ELCI_2022 configuration.

This process was created with ElectricityLCI (https://github.com/USEPA/ElectricityLCI) version 2.0.0 using the ELCI_2022 configuration.

Category: 31-33: Manufacturing/3241: Petroleum and Coal Products Manufacturing
Name: petroleum extraction and processing - DFO PADD 1
Description:
The cradle-to-gate inventory for production of refinery products (residual fuel oil or diesel fuel oil) for each U.S. Petroleum Administration for Defense District (PADD). The method for generating the inventory largely follows the method detailed in the journal article below, but in this case 2016 data were used for determining crude mixes new versions of the model were used. Additionally, OPGEE was used to provide only the amounts of various fuels used in the production of crude, and NETL unit processes were used for combustion of those fuels to provide a more complete inventory.
https://pubs.acs.org/doi/abs/10.1021/acs.est.6b02819
The model is constructed to provide the inventory for diesel fuel oil or residual fuel oil based on PADD. This inventory includes crude extraction (foreign and domestic), crude transportation, crude refining, and fuel transportation.  This process was created with ElectricityLCI (https://github.com/USEPA/ElectricityLCI) version 2.0.0 using the ELCI_2022 configuration.

Description: The cradle-to-gate inventory for the construction of the fossil fuel generators (coal, gas, and oil). The inventories represent the construction of either natural gas combined cycle or coal power plants using an economic input output life cycle inventory model (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jclepro.2017.04.150) and the construction costs for these plants as detailed by NETL techno-economics analysis (https://www.netl.doe.gov/energy-analysis/details?id=729). These type different types of power plants are then assigned to the various power plants according to the plant they most resemble. The inventories are then scaled according to power plant capacity relative to the source power plants and the allocated over an assumed 30 year life. The single year of impacts are then divided by the generation of the year selected in the model configuration. This process was created with ElectricityLCI (https://github.com/USEPA/ElectricityLCI) version 2.0.0 using the ELCI_2022 configuration.

Category: 23: Construction/2371: Utility System Construction
Name: power plant construction - solar_pv_const - Public Service Company of Colorado
Description: Solar inventories are provided in two parts - construction and from operations. Together they represent the cradle-to-gate inventories for electricity production from US solar photovoltaic power plants. Inventories were developed for each solar PV plant in the 2016 or 2020 EIA 860 data, as specified in the model configuration, using a custom life cycle model. Boundaries include plant construction and minor operating emissions from fuel combustion, etc. The total inventory for the plant construction is allocated over an assumed 30 year life. The resulting yearly construction emissions are divided by the generation for the specified year to provide inventories per MWh (e.g., kg CO2/MWh). This process was created with ElectricityLCI (https://github.com/USEPA/ElectricityLCI) version 2.0.0 using the ELCI_2022 configuration.

Category: 31-33: Manufacturing/3241: Petroleum and Coal Products Manufacturing
Name: petroleum extraction and processing - RFO PADD 5
Description:
The cradle-to-gate inventory for production of refinery products (residual fuel oil or diesel fuel oil) for each U.S. Petroleum Administration for Defense District (PADD). The method for generating the inventory largely follows the method detailed in the journal article below, but in this case 2016 data were used for determining crude mixes new versions of the model were used. Additionally, OPGEE was used to provide only the amounts of various fuels used in the production of crude, and NETL unit processes were used for combustion of those fuels to provide a more complete inventory.
https://pubs.acs.org/doi/abs/10.1021/acs.est.6b02819
The model is constructed to provide the inventory for diesel fuel oil or residual fuel oil based on PADD. This inventory includes crude extraction (foreign and domestic), crude transportation, crude refining, and fuel transportation.  This process was created with ElectricityLCI (https://github.com/USEPA/ElectricityLCI) version 2.0.0 using the ELCI_2022 configuration.

Category: 31-33: Manufacturing/3251: Basic Chemical Manufacturing
Name: nuclear fuel extraction, processing, and transport
Description:
This process represents the cradle-to-gate inventory for the production of electricity from US generation II nuclear reactors, including uranium extraction, enrichment, fuel fabrication, fuel transport, power plant construction, and power plant emissions. Documentation for the original model is available at http://www.netl.doe.gov/energy-analysis/details?id=620. This inventory represents an update to some portions of the model to represent 2016 operations, namely the retirement of US gaseous diffusion enrichment operations and to a lesser extent an updated mix of nuclear fuel sources.
The inventory is on the basis of 1 MWh generated electricity and, as a result, is the same for all nuclear power plants. This process was created with ElectricityLCI (https://github.com/USEPA/ElectricityLCI) version 2.0.0 using the ELCI_2022 configuration.

The method for generating the inventory largely follows the method detailed in the journal article below, but in this case 2016 data were used for determining crude mixes new versions of the model were used. Additionally, OPGEE was used to provide only the amounts of various fuels used in the production of crude, and NETL unit processes were used for combustion of those fuels to provide a more complete inventory.
https://pubs.acs.org/doi/abs/10.1021/acs.est.6b02819
The model is constructed to provide the inventory for diesel fuel oil or residual fuel oil based on PADD. This inventory includes crude extraction (foreign and domestic), crude transportation, crude refining, and fuel transportation.  This process was created with ElectricityLCI (https://github.com/USEPA/ElectricityLCI) version 2.0.0 using the ELCI_2022 configuration.

Category: 23: Construction/2371: Utility System Construction
Name: power plant construction - wind_const - Avista Corporation
Description: Wind inventories are provided in two parts - construction and from operations. Together they represent the cradle-to-gate inventories for electricity production from US wind turbine power plants. Inventories were developed for each wind farm in the 2016 or 2020 EIA 860 data, as specified in the model configuration, using a custom life cycle model. Boundaries include wind turbine construction and minor operating emissions from fuel combustion, maintenance, etc. The inventory for the wind farm is allocated over an assumed 30 year life. The aggregation performed in the electricity LCI code will then divide those inventories by the generation in the selected year to provide results on the basis of MWh. This process was created with ElectricityLCI (https://github.com/USEPA/ElectricityLCI) version 2.0.0 using the ELCI_2022 configuration.

Category: 23: Construction/2371: Utility System Construction
Name: power plant construction - ngcc_const - ISO New England Inc.
Description: The cradle-to-gate inventory for the construction of the fossil fuel generators (coal, gas, and oil). The inventories represent the construction of either natural gas combined cycle or coal power plants using an economic input output life cycle inventory model (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jclepro.2017.04.150) and the construction costs for these plants as detailed by NETL techno-economics analysis (https://www.netl.doe.gov/energy-analysis/details?id=729). These type different types of power plants are then assigned to the various power plants according to the plant they most resemble. The inventories are then scaled according to power plant capacity relative to the source power plants and the allocated over an assumed 30 year life. The single year of impacts are then divided by the generation of the year selected in the model configuration. This process was created with ElectricityLCI (https://github.com/USEPA/ElectricityLCI) version 2.0.0 using the ELCI_2022 configuration.

This process was created with ElectricityLCI (https://github.com/USEPA/ElectricityLCI) version 2.0.0 using the ELCI_2022 configuration.

Category: 56: Administrative and Support and Waste Management and Remediation Services/5622: Waste Treatment and Disposal
Name: Sludge hauling and landfill; wastewater treatment unit; Level 1
Description:
This gate-to gate process reflects landfill disposal of biosolids generated during the treatment of 1 cubic meter of wastewater by the (linked) conventional plug flow activated sludge, Level 1 treatment system. Inventory data was generated for a theortical process configuration treating on average 10 million gallons per day of wastewater. Key information is available in process metadata. Full process documentation is available in EPA report number 832-R-21-006A.

System boundaries of the disposal process include biosolids transport, basic infrastructure required for sludge storage at the WWTP, landfill fugitive methane emissions, emissions of toxic organics and heavy metals in landfill leachate,  and the option to include an avoided electricity benefit associated with energy recovery of landfill gas.

Category: 56: Administrative and Support and Waste Management and Remediation Services/5622: Waste Treatment and Disposal
Name: Sludge hauling and landfill; wastewater treatment unit; Level 3-2
Description:
This gate-to gate process reflects landfill disposal of biosolids generated during the treatment of 1 cubic meter of wastewater by the (linked) Modified University of Cape Town, Level 3-2 treatment system. Inventory data was generated for a theortical process configuration treating on average 10 million gallons per day of wastewater. Key information is available in process metadata. Full process documentation is available in EPA report number 832-R-21-006A.

System boundaries of the disposal process include biosolids transport, basic infrastructure required for sludge storage at the WWTP, landfill fugitive methane emissions, emissions of toxic organics and heavy metals in landfill leachate,  and the option to include an avoided electricity benefit associated with energy recovery of landfill gas.

Description: This dataset represents the movement of goods via a Single Unit Truck using Compressed Natural Gas (CNG) as a fuel source. Emissions and fuel consumption data are calculated using EPA's Motor Vehicle Emission Simulator (MOVES), the regulatory model to determine emission inventories for highway vehicles, including cars, trucks, and buses. The reference flow is the movement of 1 metric ton of goods for a distance of 1 km.

Description: This dataset represents the movement of goods via a Single Unit Truck, Short-Haul using Compressed Natural Gas (CNG) as a fuel source. Emissions and fuel consumption data are calculated using EPA's Motor Vehicle Emission Simulator (MOVES), the regulatory model to determine emission inventories for highway vehicles, including cars, trucks, and buses. The reference flow is the movement of 1 metric ton of goods for a distance of 1 km.

The process is normalized to the functional unit of  1 kg mortar production.

Category: 22: Utilities/2213: Water, Sewage and Other Systems
Name: Drinking water treatment; Pre-Disinfection Processes, no Adsorption; at plant
Description:
This process aggregates Pre-Disinfection drinking water treatment processes normalized to the functional unit of 1000 cubic feet of treated drinking water. These treatment processes include: chemical condition, sand filtration, and sedimentation. When part of the linked drinking water treatment system, this process "meets or exceeds [U.S.] National Primary Drinking Water Regulations for microorganisms, disinfectants, disinfectant by-products, inorganics, organics, and radionuclides."

To model the full scope of impacts associated with drinking water treatment - which includes acquisition, treatment, distribution and infrastructure - use one of the four drinking water processes:
• Drinking water; Chlorine disinfection; at consumer; ft3 drinking water
• Drinking water; Chlorine disinfection, no adsorption; at consumer; ft3 drinking water
• Drinking water; UV disinfection; at consumer; ft3 drinking water
• Drinking water; UV disinfection, no adsorption; at consumer; ft3 drinking water

Inventory data was collected from the Greater Cincinnati Water Works (GCWW) Richard Miller Treatment Plant, which serves a population of 241,000 customers and treats approximately 106 million gallons per day.

Category: 22: Utilities/2213: Water, Sewage and Other Systems
Name: Wastewater treatment; 5-Stage bardenpho membrane bioreactor with sidestream reverse osmosis, Level 5-2; at plant
Description:
This gate-to gate process reflects treatment of 1 cubic meter of wastewater, to the following specification:

Influent wastewater quality: Suspended solids - 220 mg/L, Biological oxygen demand - 220 mg/L, Total nitrogen - 40 mg/L N, Total phosphorus - 5 mg/L. 

Level 5 Effluent quality: Suspended solids - 1.9 mg/L, Biological oxygen demand - 0.62 mg/L, Total nitrogen - 2 mg/L, Total phosphorus - 0.02 mg/L

Inventory data was generated for a theortical process configuration treating on average 10 million gallons per day of wastewater. Key information is available in process metadata. Full process documentation is available in EPA report number 832-R-21-006A.

System boundaries of the treatment process include energy, chemical, consumable inputs and greenhouse gas emissions for major primary, secondary, tertiary, disinfection and solids treatment processes. Release of treated effluent to surface water and landfill disposal of generated sludge are modeled in separate (linked) processes. Basic infrastructure is also included (excavation, concrete, steel). Operation of the sewage collection system, infrastructure disposal, and electrical and mechanical equipment (e.g. pumps body, control boxes, clarifier scraper arm) are outside of the system boundaries. 

Wastewater treatment processes are available for five levels of treatment performance. Level one is the lowest standard. Level five produces the cleanest effluent.

Category: 31-33: Manufacturing/3251: Basic Chemical Manufacturing
Name: Spodumene mineral concentrate; beneficiation process; at plant; 5% lithium oxide
Description:
This unit process produces a mineral concentrate from hard rock (spodumene) ores using mineral processing processes. The functional unit of the process is the production of 5% lithium oxide (Li2O) mineral concentrate that will be sent to a hydrometallurgical plant for further processing. The gate-to-gate system boundary starts with the input of a mill feed with 1.89% Li2O and ends with the production of a chemical concentrate with 5% Li2O. The process does not have co-products and all the environmental impacts are allocated to mineral concentrate production.

The input mill feed has 95% solids and solids have following composition by weight: LiO2:1.89%, Al2O3:16.0%, SiO2:73.3%, Fe2O3: 0.47%, Na2O: 2.36%, K2O: 2.78%, P2O5: 0.2%. The input mill feed undergoes numerous operations including crushing, dense media separation, grinding, desliming and mica and spodumene flotation circuits to produce a chemical concentrate . Most water requirements of the process are fulfilled with recycled water from the concentrate and tails thickeners and the process needs energy and chemicals for multiple operations. This process was modelled in excel to generate the mass and energy balances required to calculate the inventory.

Category: 31-33: Manufacturing/3259: Other Chemical Product and Preparation Manufacturing
Name: Recycled postconsumer high-density polyethylene (HDPE) flake; at plant
Description:
This unit process LCI represents additional separation and processing of postconsumer resin by a reclaimer to convert the received material into clean resin ready for use in manufacturing. The system boundary includes incoming material transport, debaling of incoming materials, grinding, washing, drying, and extrusion. Pelletizing energy usage is included in a separate unit process. Incoming HDPE material contains small amounts of non-HDPE saleable material as well as unusable contaminants. The weighted average percentage of non-HDPE saleable material recovered from incoming bales was less than 1%, while 17% of the incoming material was unusable contaminants. 

Note that input collection and MRF sorting processes will be updated during the next quarterly release of USLCI,

Category: 22: Utilities/2213: Water, Sewage and Other Systems
Name: Effluent release; from conventional plug flow activated sludge, Level 1 wastewater treatment plant
Description:
This gate-to gate process reflects the pollutant emissions associated with the release of 1 cubic meter of wastewater treated to the following specification:

Level 1 Effluent quality: Suspended solids - 20 mg/L, Biological oxygen demand - 7.7 mg/L, Total nitrogen - 30 mg/L, Total phosphorus - 4.9 mg/L

Inventory data was generated for a theortical process configuration treating on average 10 million gallons per day of wastewater. Key information is available in process metadata. Full process documentation is available in EPA report number 832-R-21-006A.

This process only reflects pollutant emissions and must be utilized with the associated wastewater treatment process and sludge disposal process to reflect the full scope of wastewater treatment impacts. Reported emissions to water include solids, biological and chemical oxygen demand, nutrients, heavy metals, disinfection by-products, and a subset of toxic organic compounds. The process also reports nitrous oxide emissions associated with the release of nitrogen compounds to surface water.

Category: 31-33: Manufacturing/3251: Basic Chemical Manufacturing
Name: Lithium hydroxide monohydrate; production from pre-treated lithium brine; at plant; 100% purity
Description:
This unit process produces lithium hydroxide from pre-treated lithium brine using adsorption based direct lithium extraction (DLE) technology. The functional unit of the process is the production of 1 kg of high purity lithium hydroxide monohydrate (LiOH.H20) crystals. The gate-to-gate system boundary starts with the input of pre-treated lithium brine with 57 ppm lithum content and ends with the production high purity LiOH.H2O. The process does not have co-products and all the environmental impacts are allocated to LiOH.H2O production.

Lithium is removed from brine solution using adsroption onto zeolite based adsorbant. Lithium lean brine solution is re-injected back to the brine reservoir. Lithium is then removed using elution with dilute sulfuric acid to generate Li2SO4 solution. Mn, Mg and Ca are removed, reverse osmosis is used to concentrate Li2SO4 solution. Electrodialysis is used to produce LiOH and mechanical vapor compression crystallization to remove excess water and to generate high purity LiOH.H2O. Most water requirements of the process are fullfilled with recycled water and process needs electricity and chemicals for multiple operations. The process generates Mn/Mg/Ca-containing solid waste, which needs to be appropriately disposed. This process was simulated in SysCad simulation tool to generate the mass and energy balances required to calculate the inventory.

Category: 21: Mining, Quarrying, and Oil and Gas Extraction/2123: Nonmetallic Mineral Mining and Quarrying
Name: Lithium brine; extraction and reinjection; at plant
Description: This dataset represents lithium rich brine extraction from underground aquifer and reinjection of lithium lean brine into the aquifer. It is suitable for use with direct lithium extraction technologies (DLE), in which lithium is extracted from brine and lithium lean brine is returned to the aquifer. The functional unit of the process is the extraction and reinjection of 1 kg of lithium brine. The system boundary includes brine extraction and reinjection wells and associated pumps. In addition to electricity consumption, the resources extracted and emissions to water are included in the unit process as a difference between extraction and reinjection brine flows.

Level 1 Effluent quality: Suspended solids - 20 mg/L, Biological oxygen demand - 7.7 mg/L, Total nitrogen - 30 mg/L, Total phosphorus - 4.9 mg/L

Inventory data was generated for a theortical process configuration treating on average 10 million gallons per day of wastewater. Key information is available in process metadata. Full process documentation is available in EPA report number 832-R-21-006A.

System boundaries of the treatment process include energy, chemical, consumable inputs and greenhouse gas emissions for major primary, secondary, tertiary, disinfection and solids treatment processes. Release of treated effluent to surface water and landfill disposal of generated sludge are modeled in separate (linked) processes. Basic infrastructure is also included (excavation, concrete, steel). Operation of the sewage collection system, infrastructure disposal, and electrical and mechanical equipment (e.g. pumps body, control boxes, clarifier scraper arm) are outside of the system boundaries. 

Wastewater treatment processes are available for five levels of treatment performance. Level one is the lowest standard. Level five produces the cleanest effluent.

Category: 22: Utilities/2213: Water, Sewage and Other Systems
Name: Drinking water treatment; U.S. Average Treatment; at consumer
Description: This process reflects U.S. national average treatment of 1 cubic meter of drinking water. The national average is based on disinfection with chlorine.

Category: 31-33: Manufacturing/3251: Basic Chemical Manufacturing
Name: Lithium hydroxide monohydrate; production from spodumene concentrate; at plant; 100% purity
Description:
This unit process produces lithium hydroxide from hard rock (spodumene) mineral concentrate using hydrometallurgical process. The functional unit of the process is the production of 1 kg of pure lithium hydroxide monohydrate (LiOH.H20) crystals with 2% moisture content. The gate-to-gate system boundary starts with the input of spodumene mineral concentrate with 5% Li2O and ends with the production high purity LiOH.H2O. Transport of minreal concentrate by truck and then rail has also been included. The process does not have co-products and all the environmental impacts are allocated to LiOH.H2O production.

The input mineral concentrate has 89% solids and solids have following composition by weight: Li2O:5%, Al2O3:23.5%, SiO2:66.9%, Fe2O3: 0.4%, Na2O: 0.4%, K2O: 0.6%, P2O5: 0.1% and other:3%. The input mineral concentrate undergoes numerous opreations including calciner, acid plug mixer, rotary kiln, leaching, neutralization, Ca/Mg precipitation, reverse osmosis, electrodialysis, crystalization and drying to produce high purity LiOH.H2O. Most water requirements of the process are fullfilled with recycled water and process needs energy and chemicals for multiple operations. CO2 is emitted in the calciner operation. The tails produced contains predominantly SiO2, Al2O3 and CaSO6H4, which needs to be disposed in secure tailing ponds. This process was simulated in MetSim simulation tool to generate the mass and energy balances required to calculate the inventory.

Category: 31-33: Manufacturing/3259: Other Chemical Product and Preparation Manufacturing
Name: Recycled postconsumer polypropylene (PP) pellet; at plant
Description:
This unit process LCI represents additional separation and processing of postconsumer polypropylene (PP) resin by a reclaimer to convert the received material into clean resin ready for use in manufacturing. The system boundary includes incoming material transport, debaling of incoming materials, grinding, washing, drying, extrusion, and pelletizing. Incoming PP material contains small amounts of non-PP saleable material as well as unusable contaminants. The weighted average percentage of non-PP saleable material recovered from incoming bales was less than 1%, while 17% of the incoming material was unusable contaminants. 

Because only three facilities provided data on PP recycling, and not all the facilities converted clean flake to pellet, limited details can be provided about PP reclaimer operations in order to protect confidentiality of individual reclaimer data. Because less than three reclaimers reported converting flake to pellet, it is not possible to show separate weighted averages for clean flake processing and pelletizing of clean flake. Therefore, this process represents bale to flake to pellet conversion.

Note that input collection and MRF sorting processes will be updated during the next quarterly release of USLCI,

Category: 22: Utilities/2213: Water, Sewage and Other Systems
Name: Effluent release; from anaerobic/anoxic/oxic, Level 2-1 wastewater treatment plant
Description:
This gate-to gate process reflects the pollutant emissions associated with the release of 1 cubic meter of wastewater treated to the following specification:

Level 2 Effluent quality: Suspended solids - 20 mg/L, Biological oxygen demand - 4.7 mg/L, Total nitrogen - 8 mg/L, Total phosphorus - 0.28 mg/L

Inventory data was generated for a theortical process configuration treating on average 10 million gallons per day of wastewater. Key information is available in process metadata. Full process documentation is available in EPA report number 832-R-21-006A.

This process only reflects pollutant emissions and must be utilized with the associated wastewater treatment process and sludge disposal process to reflect the full scope of wastewater treatment impacts. Reported emissions to water include solids, biological and chemical oxygen demand, nutrients, heavy metals, disinfection by-products, and a subset of toxic organic compounds. The process also reports nitrous oxide emissions associated with the release of nitrogen compounds to surface water.

Category: 22: Utilities/2213: Water, Sewage and Other Systems
Name: Drinking water treatment; Lime Addition; at plant
Description:
This process reflects operation of the Lime Addition drinking water treatment process normalized to the functional unit of 1000 cubic feet of treated drinking water. When part of the linked drinking water treatment system, this process "meets or exceeds [U.S.] National Primary Drinking Water Regulations for microorganisms, disinfectants, disinfectant by-products, inorganics, organics, and radionuclides."

To model the full scope of impacts associated with drinking water treatment - which includes acquisition, treatment, distribution and infrastructure - use one of the four drinking water processes:
• Drinking water; Chlorine disinfection; at consumer; ft3 drinking water
• Drinking water; Chlorine disinfection, no adsorption; at consumer; ft3 drinking water
• Drinking water; UV disinfection; at consumer; ft3 drinking water
• Drinking water; UV disinfection, no adsorption; at consumer; ft3 drinking water

Inventory data was collected from the Greater Cincinnati Water Works (GCWW) Richard Miller Treatment Plant, which serves a population of 241,000 customers and treats approximately 106 million gallons per day.

Category: 31-33: Manufacturing/3273: Cement and Concrete Product Manufacturing
Name: Ready-mix concrete; at plant; 5000-50-FASL
Description:
This cradle-to-gate unit process dataset is for the production of Ready-mix concrete; at plant; 5000-50-FASL.
The product name is represented by the specific compressive strength in psi, followed by the quantity, as a percentage of the binder of either fly ash (FA), slag cement (SL), or both (FA/SL). The remaining percentage correspond to ordinary Portland cement.
Note that the percentages in the name represent the following ranges:

00-FA/SL 	0-19% Fly ash or Slag
20-FA		20-29% Fly ash		
30-FA		30-39% Fly ash
40-FA		40-49% Fly ash
30-SL		30-39% Slag
40-SL		40-49% Slag
50-SL		≥50% Slag
50-FA/SL	≥20% Fly ash and ≥30% Slag 

LW at the beginning of the name stands for Lightweight, and refers to concretes made with lightweight aggregates. 
This unit process includes raw material acquisition; manufacturing; transport; production and use of fuels, and electricity; disposal of process waste; and ancillary materials manufacturing.
According to  Athena Sustainable Materials Institute (2022), this model comprises the stages of raw material supply, transportation, and manufacturing. The latter refers to the storage, movement, batching and mixing of concrete, the operation of the concrete plant, and the transportation and processing of waste from said core processes.
“[This is] a general-purpose concrete as used in residential, commercial and public works applications in the US and Canada” (Athena Sustainable Materials, 2022)

This cradle-to-gate unit process dataset is for the production of Ready-mix concrete; at plant; 5000-30-FA.
The product name is represented by the specific compressive strength in psi, followed by the quantity, as a percentage of the binder of either fly ash (FA), slag cement (SL), or both (FA/SL). The remaining percentage correspond to ordinary Portland cement.
Note that the percentages in the name represent the following ranges:

00-FA/SL 	0-19% Fly ash or Slag
20-FA		20-29% Fly ash		
30-FA		30-39% Fly ash
40-FA		40-49% Fly ash
30-SL		30-39% Slag
40-SL		40-49% Slag
50-SL		≥50% Slag
50-FA/SL	≥20% Fly ash and ≥30% Slag 

LW at the beginning of the name stands for Lightweight, and refers to concretes made with lightweight aggregates. 
This unit process includes raw material acquisition; manufacturing; transport; production and use of fuels, and electricity; disposal of process waste; and ancillary materials manufacturing.
According to  Athena Sustainable Materials Institute (2022), this model comprises the stages of raw material supply, transportation, and manufacturing. The latter refers to the storage, movement, batching and mixing of concrete, the operation of the concrete plant, and the transportation and processing of waste from said core processes.
“[This is] a general-purpose concrete as used in residential, commercial and public works applications in the US and Canada” (Athena Sustainable Materials, 2022)

Category: Dummy waste treatment
Name: Incineration of used oil and oily waste; incineration at cement kiln; at treatment plant (dummy)
Description:
This is a dummy process for the incineration of waste oil and oily waste in a cement kiln. It does not contain any inputs or emissions, and thus no allocation is currently necessary.
The inputs are the cutoff flows "Waste, oil, combusted in industrial boiler" and "Disposal, oil waste, to unspecified treatment", from the USLCI database, and the cutoff output was specfically created for the NIST Construction Materials database. 
The density of waste oil is taken from Technical Working Group of the Basel Convention (2002), and it is merely added as a reference for future work.

Category: Bridge Processes/Construction Materials to USLCI
Name: Antifreeze Bridge; Construction Materials to USLCI
Description:
This process links an input in a Construction Materials process to a provider from the USLCI repository on the Federal LCA Commons. The intended provider for this process is "Ethylene glycol, materials production, organic compound, at plant, kg - RNA"
According to the National Library of Medicine, ethylene glycol is used to make antifreeze (see link below) and therefore is used here as a proxy for those inventories where "antifreeze" is an input flow, but who give no additional information on the composition of the antifreeze used.

https://pubchem.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/compound/Ethylene-Glycol

Category: 31-33: Manufacturing/3273: Cement and Concrete Product Manufacturing
Name: Masonry cement; production mix, at plant
Description:
This cradle-to-gate unit process dataset is for the production of 1 metric ton of masonry cement.
It includes raw material acquisition; processing; transport; production and use of fuels and electricity; disposal of process waste; recovery of used products via reuse; and ancillary materials manufacturing.
Athena Sustainable Materials Institute (2021) did not specify which activities were included in this process. However, based on their Figure 2 it can be inferred that blending, cement grinding in the cement silo are all part of this process. The production of clinker had its own inventory, upon which the model “Clinker; production mix, at plant”, available in this database, is based.
Masonry cement is a hydraulic cement manufactured for its use in mortars for masonry construction, in plasters, on in both. It contains a plasticizing, and possible other performance-enhancing additions (Athena Sustainable Materials, 2021).

Category: Bridge Processes/Construction Materials to USLCI
Name: Wastewater, onsite lagoons or septic tank, at treatment plant; Construction Materials to USLCI
Description:
This process links an input in a Construction Materials process to a provider from the USLCI repository on the Federal LCA Commons. The intended provider for this process is "Wastewater treatment; Conventional plug flow activated sludge, Level 1; at plant"
Note that the USLCI flow represent the treatment in a municipal wastewater treatment plant, and not in an industrial wastewater treatment plant specifically designed to treat the wastewater from a particular industry. Therefore, this bridge process may be consider a proxy until a industry specific wastewater treatment plant is modeled.

Category: Bridge Processes/Construction Materials to USLCI
Name: Motor oil, from petroleum Bridge; Construction Materials to USLCI
Description:
This process links an input in a Construction Materials process to a provider from the USLCI repository on the Federal LCA Commons. The intended provider for this process is "Lubricant feedstock, at refinery".
Motor oil and lubricant may be seen as synonyms, but the fact that the USLCI flow is “at refinery” and not “at plant” would suggest USLCI’s “Lubricant feedstock, at refinery" is only a proxy for “Motor oil, from refined petroleum; at plant”.
In addition, this bridge process was created to help distinguish between “Motor oil” and other petroleum derivates for whom “Lubricant feedstock, at refinery” may also be used as proxy.
Note that motor oils can be either synthetic, or made from refined petroleum (see link below).
https://thelubepage.com/amsoil-magazine-articles/magazine-articles/articles/petroleum-oil-production-and-oil-vs-synthetic-motor-oil

Category: Bridge Processes/Construction Materials to USLCI
Name: Grease, from petroleum Bridge; Construction Materials to USLCI
Description:
This process links an input in a Construction Materials process to a provider from the USLCI repository on the Federal LCA Commons. The intended provider for this process is "Lubricant feedstock, at refinery".
As for motor oil (see link below), lubricating greases can be either synthetic, or made from refined petroleum.  (NAICS codes 325998 and 324191 respectively). Therefore, USLCI "Lubricant feedstock, at refinery" may only be a good proxy the latter kind of grease.
https://thelubepage.com/amsoil-magazine-articles/magazine-articles/articles/petroleum-oil-production-and-oil-vs-synthetic-motor-oil

Conversion of 874 kg/m3 is based on an API 30 oil, according to the link below.
https://www.machinerylubrication.com/Read/29319/measuring-relative-density#:~:text=The%20density%20of%20most%20oils,they%20are%20lighter%20by%20volume.

Category: 31-33: Manufacturing/3274: Lime and Gypsum Product Manufacturing
Name: Gypsum board; at plant; 1/2" lightweight
Description:
This cradle-to-gate unit process dataset is for the production of 1 MSF (Thousand square feet, 92.0 square meters) of 1/2” Lightweight (regular core) gypsum board. 
This unit process includes raw material acquisition; manufacturing; transport; production and use of fuels, and electricity; disposal of process waste and products; recovery of used products via reuse, recycling, and energy recovery; and ancillary materials manufacturing.
"Gypsum board is the generic name for a family of sheet products consisting of a non-combustible core primarily of gypsum with a paper facing" (Athena Sustainable Materials Institute, 2020). According to that same source: "Gypsum wallboard is manufactured in a two-step process. In the first step finely crushed and ground gypsum, calcium sulfate dihydrate (CaSO4 × 2H 2O), is heated and partially dehydrated (calcined) to calcium sulfate hemihydrate (CaSO4 × 1/2H2O), called […]. In the second step of the manufacturing process the stucco is mixed with a number of additives. A foaming agent and an excess amount of water is also added to prepare a gypsum slurry which is extruded on a fast moving, board production line between two layers of gypsum paper. The “raw” gypsum board is then allowed to fully hydrate – calcium sulfate hemihydrate is converted back to dihydrate – before it is cut to the desired size and before it enters a heated kiln, where at elevated temperatures excess water is driven off. The gypsum board is then packaged and stacked, ready to be shipped).”

Category: 31-33: Manufacturing/3274: Lime and Gypsum Product Manufacturing
Name: Gypsum board; at plant; 5/8" type X
Description:
This cradle-to-gate unit process dataset is for the production of 1 MSF (Thousand square feet, 92.90 square meters) of 1/8” (15.9 mm) Type X (type X core) gypsum board. 
This unit process includes raw material acquisition; manufacturing; transport; production and use of fuels, and electricity; disposal of process waste and products; recovery of used products via reuse, recycling, and energy recovery; and ancillary materials manufacturing.
"Gypsum board is the generic name for a family of sheet products consisting of a non-combustible core primarily of gypsum with a paper facing" (Athena Sustainable Materials Institute, 2020). According to that same source: "Gypsum wallboard is manufactured in a two-step process. In the first step finely crushed and ground gypsum, calcium sulfate dihydrate (CaSO4 × 2H 2O), is heated and partially dehydrated (calcined) to calcium sulfate hemihydrate (CaSO4 × 1/2H2O), called […]. In the second step of the manufacturing process the stucco is mixed with a number of additives. A foaming agent and an excess amount of water is also added to prepare a gypsum slurry which is extruded on a fast moving, board production line between two layers of gypsum paper. The “raw” gypsum board is then allowed to fully hydrate – calcium sulfate hemihydrate is converted back to dihydrate – before it is cut to the desired size and before it enters a heated kiln, where at elevated temperatures excess water is driven off. The gypsum board is then packaged and stacked, ready to be shipped).”

Category: 31-33: Manufacturing/3279: Other Nonmetallic Mineral Product Manufacturing
Name: Slag cement; at plant
Description:
This cradle-to-gate unit process dataset is for the production of 1 short ton of slag cement. 
It includes raw material acquisition; processing; transport; production and use of fuels, and electricity; disposal of process waste; and ancillary materials manufacturing.
According to Dunlap (2003), this model encompasses the stages of (i) quenching/granulation, (ii) dewatering and/or drying, (iii) crushing, (iv) grinding, (v) storage, and (vi) shipping.
“Slag cement, also known as ground granulated blast furnace slag, is a supplementary cementitious material used as a partial replacement for Portland cement in concrete. Slag is waste material from a blast furnace during the production of pig iron. This life cycle inventory for slag cement assumes slag comes into to the system boundary with no environmental burdens since it is a waste product. To process slag so it can be used as a supplementary cementitious material in concrete, it is quenched with water and ground.” (Dunlap, 2003)

Category: 31-33: Manufacturing/3273: Cement and Concrete Product Manufacturing
Name: Blended hydraulic cement; production mix, at plant
Description:
This cradle-to-gate unit process dataset is for the production of 1 metric ton of blended hydraulic cement cement.
It includes raw material acquisition; processing; transport; production and use of fuels and electricity; disposal of process waste; recovery of used products via reuse; and ancillary materials manufacturing.
Athena Sustainable Materials Institute (2021) did not specify which activities were included in this process. However, based on their Figure 2 it can be inferred that blending, cement grinding in the cement silo are all part of this process. The production of clinker had its own inventory, upon which the model “Clinker; production mix, at plant”, available in this database, is based.
Blended hydraulic cement consist of two or more inorganic constituents, one of which is not Portland cement or Portland cement clinker, which separately or in combination contribute to the strength gaining properties of the cement (Athena Sustainable Materials, 2021). Portland-limestone cement is a blended cement, but it has is own process in this database.

It includes raw material acquisition; processing; transport; production and use of fuels and electricity; disposal of process waste; recovery of used products via reuse; and ancillary materials manufacturing.
Athena Sustainable Materials Institute (2021) did not specify which activities were included in this process. However, based on their Figure 2 it can be inferred that blending, cement grinding in the cement silo are all part of this process. The production of clinker had its own inventory, upon which the model “Clinker; production mix, at plant”, available in this database, is based.
Portland cement is “[a] hydraulic cement produced by pulverizing clinker, consisting essentially of crystalline hydraulic calcium silicates, and usually containing one or more of the following: water, calcium sulfate, up to 5% limestone, and processing additions” (Athena Sustainable Materials Institute (2021).

Category: 31-33: Manufacturing/3273: Cement and Concrete Product Manufacturing
Name: Ready-mix concrete; at plant; LW-3000-40-FA
Description:
This cradle-to-gate unit process dataset is for the production of Ready-mix concrete; at plant; LW-3000-40-FA.
The product name is represented by the specific compressive strength in psi, followed by the quantity, as a percentage of the binder of either fly ash (FA), slag cement (SL), or both (FA/SL). The remaining percentage correspond to ordinary Portland cement.
Note that the percentages in the name represent the following ranges:

00-FA/SL 	0-19% Fly ash or Slag
20-FA		20-29% Fly ash		
30-FA		30-39% Fly ash
40-FA		40-49% Fly ash
30-SL		30-39% Slag
40-SL		40-49% Slag
50-SL		≥50% Slag
50-FA/SL	≥20% Fly ash and ≥30% Slag 

LW at the beginning of the name stands for Lightweight, and refers to concretes made with lightweight aggregates. 
This unit process includes raw material acquisition; manufacturing; transport; production and use of fuels, and electricity; disposal of process waste; and ancillary materials manufacturing.
According to  Athena Sustainable Materials Institute (2022), this model comprises the stages of raw material supply, transportation, and manufacturing. The latter refers to the storage, movement, batching and mixing of concrete, the operation of the concrete plant, and the transportation and processing of waste from said core processes.
“[This is] a general-purpose concrete as used in residential, commercial and public works applications in the US and Canada” (Athena Sustainable Materials, 2022)

Category: 21: Mining, Quarrying, and Oil and Gas Extraction/2123: Nonmetallic Mineral Mining and Quarrying
Name: Gypsum ore; at quarry MX/ES
Description:
IMPORTAN NOTE: This inventory is identical to "Gypsum ore; at quarry" generated for Northern America, and used as a proxy while data for Mexico and Spain becomes available.

This cradle-to-gate unit process dataset is for the extraction and processing of 1 metric ton of gypsum ore. 
It includes raw material acquisition; manufacturing; transport; production and use of fuels, and electricity; disposal of process waste; and ancillary materials manufacturing.
According to Athena Sustainable Materials Institute (2020), “gypsum rock is open pit quarried or mined underground, generally by drilling and blasting, then moved to a primary crusher on the quarry/mine site. The quarry process begins with the removal of overburden (earth) over the gypsum deposit. The gypsum rock is then drilled and blasted loose where it is then extracted and transported to the primary crusher. At the primary crusher the gypsum rock is reduced to approximately 2” (50 mm) to 5” (125 mm) or less in size. From here the crushed rock can be sent to the gypsum board manufacturing plant (trucked or belt conveyed) for secondary crushing. It may be also be transported by ship, or truck to board manufacturing plants farther away”.

Category: 31-33: Manufacturing/3222: Converted Paper Product Manufacturing
Name: Gypsum facing paper; at plant
Description:
This cradle-to-gate unit process dataset is for the production of 1 MSF (Thousand square feet, 92.0 square meters) of gypsum packing paper.
It includes raw material acquisition; manufacturing; transport; production and use of fuels, and electricity; disposal of process waste and products; recovery of used products via reuse, recycling, and energy recovery; and ancillary materials manufacturing.
According to Athena Sustainable Materials Institute (2020): "The input materials used for gypsum paper facings (known as facing and backing gypsum paper) are from recycled sources, either pre-consumer (known as post-industrial) or post-consumer. The recycled paper consists of Old Corrugated Container (OCC), Double-Lined Kraft Corrugated Cuttings (DLK), and mixed recovered papers (mixed waste papers/flyleaves, signature/white news blank, pub blank, coated fly etc.). The manufacture of gypsum paper facings is similar to other paper making processes and starts with the recycled paper furnish being fed into a pulper, a large blender that disintegrates and dissolves the old paper into a pulp – a slurry of paper fibers. The paper slurry is also cleaned of various contaminants (e.g., bailing wire, staples, and glue) before it is fed into the paper-making machine. Two types of paper making equipment – rotating cylinders or a Fourdrinier flat wire machine, may be used to produce gypsum paper. A cylinder machine rotates a large drum through the pulp slurry vat. A wide felt belt passes over the top of the turning drum cylinder. The cylinder pulls the pulp up and presses it against the bottom felt, where it sticks to form a single ply of paper – depending on the machine and slurry composition it can take as few as three to up to nine cylinder made plies pressed together to make a continuous sheet of gypsum board paper. The characteristics of the pulp entering the vats determine whether the system produces cream stock, often called “ivory”, used for the face of the gypsum board or gray stock, which makes the backside paper of gypsum wallboard. The gray stock is derived exclusively from OCC, while the cream or ivory uses a combination of all recycled inputs for its pulp plies. Instead of a set of cylinder machines, the Fourdrinier uses two machines to make a two-ply paper with the same characteristics as the multi-ply cylinder made paper. The pulp slurry is systematically fed onto a continuous running wire screen (the Fourdrinier). As the screen moves forward, water drains from the pulp through the screen to create paper. One Fourdrinier machine makes the surface (top) ply, which may be cream or gray stock depending on the pulp mixture and the desired paper type. The second machine produces a gray (bottom) ply. From this point, both systems operate in the same way. In the press section, the paper plies are pressed together to squeeze out excess water. Next the paper enters a series of high temperature dryers where any remaining water is removed. The dry paper is then sent to a calendar stack, where different chemicals or treatments are applied (e.g., retention chemicals and sizing agents). The paper is then gathered on a roll, trimmed and packaged for shipment."

Category: 21: Mining, Quarrying, and Oil and Gas Extraction/2123: Nonmetallic Mineral Mining and Quarrying
Name: Aggregates; with explosives; at quarry
Description:
This cradle-to-gate unit process dataset is for the production of 1 short ton of natural aggregates, extracted with the use of explosives.
It includes raw material acquisition; processing; transport; production and use of fuels and electricity; and ancillary materials manufacturing.
According to Miller and Ciavola (2024), this model encompasses the stages of raw material supply, transport and production. Note that due to the nature of the process, it is during production that the extraction of natural rock takes place. This includes the activities of blasting, dredging, and digging. Following extraction, processing takes place, which may include belt transport, crushing, screening, washing, and water reclamation. Transport (within the facility or to a secondary one) and stockpiling are the final steps of the production process.

Name: Ready-mix concrete; U.S. national benchmark mix design, at plant; 5000 LW
Description:
This cradle-to-gate unit process dataset is for the production of Ready-mix concrete; U.S. national benchmark mix design, at plant; 5000 LW.
The product name is represented by the specific compressive strength in psi, followed by the quantity, as a percentage of the binder of either fly ash (FA), slag cement (SL), or both (FA/SL). The remaining percentage correspond to ordinary Portland cement.
Note that the percentages in the name represent the following ranges:

00-FA/SL 	0-19% Fly ash or Slag
20-FA		20-29% Fly ash		
30-FA		30-39% Fly ash
40-FA		40-49% Fly ash
30-SL		30-39% Slag
40-SL		40-49% Slag
50-SL		≥50% Slag
50-FA/SL	≥20% Fly ash and ≥30% Slag 

LW at the beginning of the name stands for Lightweight, and refers to concretes made with lightweight aggregates. 
This unit process includes raw material acquisition; manufacturing; transport; production and use of fuels, and electricity; disposal of process waste; and ancillary materials manufacturing.
According to  Athena Sustainable Materials Institute (2022), this model comprises the stages of raw material supply, transportation, and manufacturing. The latter refers to the storage, movement, batching and mixing of concrete, the operation of the concrete plant, and the transportation and processing of waste from said core processes.
“[This is] a general-purpose concrete as used in residential, commercial and public works applications in the US and Canada” (Athena Sustainable Materials, 2022)

Category: 21: Mining, Quarrying, and Oil and Gas Extraction/2123: Nonmetallic Mineral Mining and Quarrying
Name: Aggregates; without explosives; at quarry
Description:
This cradle-to-gate unit process dataset is for the production of 1 short ton of aggregates, extracted without the use of explosives. 
It includes raw material acquisition; processing; transport; production and use of fuels and electricity; and ancillary materials manufacturing.
According to, Miller and Ciavola (2024), this model encompasses the stages of raw material supply, transport and production. Note that due to the nature of the process, it is during production that the extraction of natural rock takes place. This includes the activities of dredging and digging. Following extraction, processing takes place, which may include belt transport, crushing, screening, washing, and water reclamation. Transport (within the facility or to a secondary one) and stockpiling are the final steps of the production process.

Category: 31-33: Manufacturing/3251: Basic Chemical Manufacturing
Name: Ball mill accelerator; at plant
Description:
This cradle-to-gate unit process dataset is for the production of 1 kg of ball mil accelerator, a product to control the set of wall board core during gypsum board manufacturing (National Gypsum Company, 2006). 
This unit process only includes raw material acquisition, since this is not a full life cycle inventory, but an estimation of the composition of this product based on the referenced material safety datasheet (SDS).

Category: 31-33: Manufacturing/3273: Cement and Concrete Product Manufacturing
Name: Portland limestone cement; production mix, at plant
Description:
This cradle-to-gate unit process dataset is for the production of 1 metric ton of Portland limestone cement.
It includes raw material acquisition; processing; transport; production and use of fuels and electricity; disposal of process waste; recovery of used products via reuse; and ancillary materials manufacturing.
Athena Sustainable Materials Institute (2021) did not specify which activities were included in this process. However, based on their Figure 2 it can be inferred that blending, cement grinding in the cement silo are all part of this process. The production of clinker had its own inventory, upon which the model “Clinker; production mix, at plant”, available in this database, is based.
Portland limestone cement is a blended hydraulic cement in which the limestone content is more than 5 %, but less than or equal to 15 % (Athena Sustainable Materials, 2021).

Category: 21: Mining, Quarrying, and Oil and Gas Extraction/2123: Nonmetallic Mineral Mining and Quarrying
Name: Crushed gravel; at quarry
Description:
This cradle-to-gate unit process dataset is for the production of 1 kg of crushed gravel (Kies gebrochen). 
It includes raw material acquisition; manufacturing; transport; production and use of fuels, and electricity; and ancillary materials manufacturing.
According to Künniger et al. (2000), the model encompasses  the stages of Mining (Materialabbau), raw material transport (Rohmaterialdepot), screening (Siebmachine), barrel washing (Waschtrommel), crushing (Brecher), storage (Silo), dumping (Siloabzug), and washing (Nachwaschanlage).

Category: 21: Mining, Quarrying, and Oil and Gas Extraction/2123: Nonmetallic Mineral Mining and Quarrying
Name: Gypsum ore; at quarry
Description:
This cradle-to-gate unit process dataset is for the extraction and processing of 1 metric ton of gypsum ore. 
It includes raw material acquisition; manufacturing; transport; production and use of fuels, and electricity; disposal of process waste; and ancillary materials manufacturing.
According to Athena Sustainable Materials Institute (2020), “gypsum rock is open pit quarried or mined underground, generally by drilling and blasting, then moved to a primary crusher on the quarry/mine site. The quarry process begins with the removal of overburden (earth) over the gypsum deposit. The gypsum rock is then drilled and blasted loose where it is then extracted and transported to the primary crusher. At the primary crusher the gypsum rock is reduced to approximately 2” (50 mm) to 5” (125 mm) or less in size. From here the crushed rock can be sent to the gypsum board manufacturing plant (trucked or belt conveyed) for secondary crushing. It may be also be transported by ship, or truck to board manufacturing plants farther away”.

Category: 11: Agriculture, Forestry, Fishing and Hunting/1133: Logging
Name: Roundwood, hardwood; average harvesting; at forest road
Description:
This gate-to-gate process is for 1 m3 of hardwood harvested, extracted, and processed at the landing site. Harvested roundwood is ready to be transported for further processing.

There was no nursery and tree planting stage in the system boundary as natural regeneration is more common in the management of hardwood forests than use of plantation silviculture. The following activities are included in the system boundary: felling, skidding, processing, and loading. Input description notes describe the breakdown of inputs across these activities.

LCI inputs of fuel consumed were taken as an average of manual and mechanized harvesting in uneven-aged forests and even-aged forests. Fuel input data on individual harvesting processes is available in Table S2-S5 of Zhao et al., 2024.

Category: 31-33: Manufacturing/3211: Sawmills and Wood Preservation
Name: Sawn lumber, softwood; rough, green; at sawmill
Description: This gate-to-gate unit process is for 1 m3 of softwood lumber ready for kiln-drying. The studied system includes the logyard and sawmill processes, including transport of logs from the forest road to logyards, followed by unloading, grading, and storage. Logs are then debarked, cut into bolts, and trimmed to length. Coproducts include chips, sawdust, bark, and hog fuel.

Category: 22: Utilities/2213: Water, Sewage and Other Systems
Name: Electricity; average; onsite boiler, softwood mill
Description: This unit process represents average electricity generated from steam produced by wood fuel combustion.

Category: 31-33: Manufacturing/3211: Sawmills and Wood Preservation
Name: Sawn lumber, softwood; rough, kiln-dried; at kiln
Description: This gate-to-gate unit process is for 1 m3 of softwood lumber dried to a moisture content of 15% and ready for planing. Heat energy used in the drying process is generated on site from a wood-fired or natural gas boiler. The gas boiler produced less than 10 percent of the energy for the dryers and the wood boiler supplied the remaining 90 percent.

Category: 22: Utilities/2213: Water, Sewage and Other Systems
Name: Wood combusted; at boiler, at softwood lumber mill
Description: This unit process represents the combustion of 1 kg wood residue either generated on-site or purchased from outside the system boundary.  The energy output is steam and is used to heat the kilns that dry wood. Softwood lumber sawmill respondents provided information on how much woodfuel was used in the boiler. Of the wood fuel burned, 92 percent was produced on site and the rest was purchased from outside. Material and energy inputs for combustion of woodfuel and emissions at the boiler were updated withe data from a LCA study of wood fired boilers used in the wood products industry by Puettmann and Milota, 2017.

Description: This gate-to-gate unit process is for 1 m3 of hardwood lumber dried to a moisture content of 8% and ready for planing.

Category: 22: Utilities/2213: Water, Sewage and Other Systems
Name: Wood combusted; at boiler, at softwood lumber mill
Description:
This unit process represents the combustion of 1 kg wood residue either generated on-site or purchased from outside the system boundary.  The energy output is steam and is used to heat the kilns that dry wood. Softwood lumber sawmill respondents provided information on how much wood fuel was used in the boiler. Over 99% of the woodfuel burned was produced onsite. 

Material and energy inputs for combustion of wood fuel and emissions at the boiler were updated with data from a LCA study of wood fired boilers used in the wood products industry by Puettmann and Milota, 2017.

Category: 31-33: Manufacturing/3211: Sawmills and Wood Preservation
Name: Sawn lumber, hardwood; rough, green; at sawmill
Description: This gate-to-gate unit process is for 1 m3 of hardwood lumber broken down from logs and ready for sale or kiln-drying. Coproducts include green bark, which is sold, and green coproducts (chips, sawdust, hogged material), which is sold or used as fuel.

Category: 31-33: Manufacturing/3211: Sawmills and Wood Preservation
Name: Sawn lumber, hardwood; planed, kiln dried, packaged; at planer mill
Description: This gate-to-gate unit process is for 1 m3 of hardwood lumber shaved to uniform thickness, packaged and ready for shipment. Dry coproducts (sawdust, planar shavings, and hogged material) generated during this process are sold.

Category: 31-33: Manufacturing/3211: Sawmills and Wood Preservation
Name: Sawn lumber, hardwood; rough, kiln dried; at kiln
Description: This gate-to-gate unit process is for 1 m3 of hardwood lumber dried to a moisture content of 8% and ready for planing.

Category: Bridge Processes/Forestry and Forest Products to US Electricity Baseline
Name: Electricity Bridge; SPP - FERC; Forestry and Forest Products to US Electricity Baseline
Description: This process links an input in a Forestry and Forest Products process to a provider from the Electricity Baseline repository on the Federal LCA Commons. The intended provider for this process is "Electricity; at user; consumption mix - SPP - FERC".

Category: 31-33: Manufacturing/3211: Sawmills and Wood Preservation
Name: Sawn lumber, softwood; rough, kiln-dried; at kiln
Description: This gate-to-gate unit process is for 1 m3 of softwood lumber dried to a moisture content of 15% and ready for planing. Heat energy used in the drying process is generated on site from a wood-fired or natural gas boiler. The gas boiler produced less than 1 percent of the energy for the dryers and the wood boiler supplied the remaining 99 percent.

Category: 31-33: Manufacturing/3211: Sawmills and Wood Preservation
Name: Sawn lumber, softwood; rough, kiln-dried; at kiln
Description: This gate-to-gate unit process is for 1 m3 of softwood lumber dried to a moisture content of 15% and ready for planing. Heat energy is used in the drying process and is generated on site from a wood-fired or natural gas boiler. Gas boilers produce less than 10 percent of the energy for the dryers and wood boilers supply the remaining 90 percent for the industry.

Category: 11: Agriculture, Forestry, Fishing and Hunting/1133: Logging
Name: Roundwood, hardwood; average harvesting; at forest road
Description:
This gate-to-gate process is for 1 m3 of hardwood harvested, extracted, and processed at the landing site. Harvested roundwood is ready to be transported for further processing.

There was no nursery and tree planting stage in the system boundary as natural regeneration is more common in the management of hardwood forests than use of plantation silviculture. The following activities are included in the system boundary: felling, skidding, processing, and loading. Input description notes describe the breakdown of inputs across these activities.

LCI inputs of fuel consumed were taken as an average of manual and mechanized harvesting in uneven-aged forests and even-aged forests. Fuel input data on individual harvesting processes is available in Table S2-S5 of Zhao et al., 2024.

Category: 31-33: Manufacturing/3211: Sawmills and Wood Preservation
Name: Sawn lumber, softwood; rough, green; at sawmill
Description: This gate-to-gate unit process is for 1 m3 of softwood lumber ready for kiln-drying. The studied system includes the logyard and sawmill processes, including transport of logs from the forest road to logyards, followed by unloading, grading, and storage. Logs are then debarked, cut into bolts, and trimmed to length. Coproducts include chips, sawdust, bark, and hog fuel.

Category: 22: Utilities/2211: Electric Power Generation, Transmission and Distribution
Name: Electricity mix
Description: Electricity mix for the pacific coast region.

Category: 22: Utilities/2213: Water, Sewage and Other Systems
Name: Wood combusted; at boiler, at softwood lumber mill
Description:
This unit process represents the combustion of 1 kg wood residue either generated on-site or purchased from outside the system boundary.  The energy output is steam and is used to heat the kilns that dry wood. Softwood lumber sawmill respondents provided information on how much woodfuel was used in the boiler. Of the wood fuel burned, 94% was produced on site and the rest was purchased from outside. 

Material and energy inputs for combustion of wood fuel and emissions at the boiler were updated with data from a LCA study of wood fired boilers used in the wood products industry by Puettmann and Milota, 2017.

Category: demands
Name: Production, Complete, 2012
Description:
The final demand vectors represent purchases of goods and services by final consumers, including by households, investors and governments. "Consumption" is defined as final use within the US of all goods and services that are both produced in the US and imported from overseas. "Production" is defined as final use, either within the US or abroad, of all goods and services that are produced in the US. "Complete" indicates the consumption and production demand vectors are from the BEA Use table, while "Domestic" indicates the demand vectors are from the BEA Domestic Use table.


This USEEIO model, 2.0.1-411, was built using useeior v1.0.2 which can be found at https://github.com/USEPA/useeior/releases. More information at USEEIO is available at https://www.epa.gov/land-research/us-environmentally-extended-input-output-useeio-models.

Category: 81: Other Services (except Public Administration)/8141: Private Households
Name: Household employees
Description:
BEA Code & Name is '814000:Private households'. Industries in the Private Households subsector include private households that engage in employing workers on or about the premises in activities primarily concerned with the operation of the household. These private households may employ individuals, such as cooks, maids, butlers, and outside workers, such as gardeners, caretakers, and other maintenance workers.

This USEEIO model, 2.0.1-411, was built using useeior v1.0.2 which can be found at https://github.com/USEPA/useeior/releases. More information at USEEIO is available at https://www.epa.gov/land-research/us-environmentally-extended-input-output-useeio-models.

Category: 81: Other Services (except Public Administration)/8134: Civic and Social Organizations
Name: Civic, social, professional, and similar organizations
Description:
BEA Code & Name is '813B00:Civic, social, professional, and similar organizations'. The Other Services (except Public Administration) sector comprises establishments engaged in providing services not specifically provided for elsewhere in the classification system. Establishments in this sector are primarily engaged in activities, such as equipment and machinery repairing, promoting or administering religious activities, grantmaking, advocacy, and providing drycleaning and laundry services, personal care services, death care services, pet care services, photofinishing services, temporary parking services, and dating services.   Private households that engage in employing workers on or about the premises in activities primarily concerned with the operation of the household are included in this sector.   Excluded from this sector are establishments primarily engaged in retailing new equipment and also performing repairs and general maintenance on equipment. These establishments are classified in Sector 44-45, Retail Trade. Related NAICS codes are: 8134, 8139

This USEEIO model, 2.0.1-411, was built using useeior v1.0.2 which can be found at https://github.com/USEPA/useeior/releases. More information at USEEIO is available at https://www.epa.gov/land-research/us-environmentally-extended-input-output-useeio-models.

Name: Commercial machinery repair
Description:
BEA Code & Name is '811300:Commercial and industrial machinery and equipment repair and maintenance'. This industry comprises establishments primarily engaged in the repair and maintenance of commercial and industrial machinery and equipment. Establishments in this industry either sharpen/install commercial and industrial machinery blades and saws or provide welding (e.g., automotive, general) repair services; or repair agricultural and other heavy and industrial machinery and equipment (e.g., forklifts and other materials handling equipment, machine tools, commercial refrigeration equipment, construction equipment, and mining machinery).

Name: Electronic  equipment repair and maintenance
Description:
BEA Code & Name is '811200:Electronic and precision equipment repair and maintenance'. This industry group comprises establishments primarily engaged in repairing electronic equipment, such as computers and communications equipment, and highly specialized precision instruments. Establishments in this industry group typically have staff skilled in repairing items having complex, electronic components.

This USEEIO model, 2.0.1-411, was built using useeior v1.0.2 which can be found at https://github.com/USEPA/useeior/releases. More information at USEEIO is available at https://www.epa.gov/land-research/us-environmentally-extended-input-output-useeio-models.

Establishments in this industry group employ mechanics with specialized technical skills to diagnose and repair the mechanical and electrical systems for automotive vehicles, repair automotive interiors, and paint or repair automotive exteriors.

Name: Full-service restaurants
Description:
BEA Code & Name is '722110:Full-service restaurants'. This industry comprises establishments primarily engaged in providing food services to patrons who order and are served while seated (i.e., waiter/waitress service) and pay after eating. These establishments may provide this type of food service to patrons in combination with selling alcoholic beverages, providing carry out services, or presenting live nontheatrical entertainment.

This USEEIO model, 2.0.1-411, was built using useeior v1.0.2 which can be found at https://github.com/USEPA/useeior/releases. More information at USEEIO is available at https://www.epa.gov/land-research/us-environmentally-extended-input-output-useeio-models.

Category: 72: Accommodation and Food Services/7225: Restaurants and Other Eating Places
Name: Limited-service restaurants
Description:
BEA Code & Name is '722211:Limited-service restaurants'. This U.S. industry comprises establishments primarily engaged in providing food services (except snack and nonalcoholic beverage bars) where patrons generally order or select items and pay before eating. Food and drink may be consumed on premises, taken out, or delivered to the customer's location. Some establishments in this industry may provide these food services in combination with selling alcoholic beverages.

This USEEIO model, 2.0.1-411, was built using useeior v1.0.2 which can be found at https://github.com/USEPA/useeior/releases. More information at USEEIO is available at https://www.epa.gov/land-research/us-environmentally-extended-input-output-useeio-models.

Category: 72: Accommodation and Food Services/7223: Special Food Services
Name: All other food and drinking places
Description:
BEA Code & Name is '722A00:All other food and drinking places'. The Accommodation and Food Services sector comprises establishments providing customers with lodging and/or preparing meals, snacks, and beverages for immediate consumption. The sector includes both accommodation and food services establishments because the two activities are often combined at the same establishment.   Excluded from this sector are civic and social organizations; amusement and recreation parks; theaters; and other recreation or entertainment facilities providing food and beverage services. Related NAICS codes are: 7223-4

This USEEIO model, 2.0.1-411, was built using useeior v1.0.2 which can be found at https://github.com/USEPA/useeior/releases. More information at USEEIO is available at https://www.epa.gov/land-research/us-environmentally-extended-input-output-useeio-models.

Category: 72: Accommodation and Food Services/7211: Traveler Accommodation
Name: Hotels and campgrounds
Description:
BEA Code & Name is '721000:Accommodation'. Industries in the Accommodation subsector provide lodging or short-term accommodations for travelers, vacationers, and others. There is a wide range of establishments in these industries. Some provide lodging only; while others provide meals, laundry services, and recreational facilities, as well as lodging. Lodging establishments are classified in this subsector even if the provision of complementary services generates more revenue. The types of complementary services provided vary from establishment to establishment. The subsector is organized into three industry groups: (1) traveler accommodation, (2) recreational accommodation, and (3) rooming and boarding houses. The Traveler Accommodation industry group includes establishments that primarily provide traditional types of lodging services. This group includes hotels, motels, and bed and breakfast inns. In addition to lodging, these establishments may provide a range of other services to their guests. The RV (Recreational Vehicle) Parks and Recreational Camps industry group includes establishments that operate lodging facilities primarily designed to accommodate outdoor enthusiasts. Included are travel trailer campsites, recreational vehicle parks, and outdoor adventure retreats. The Rooming and Boarding Houses industry group includes establishments providing temporary or longer-term accommodations, which for the period of occupancy, may serve as a principal residence. Board (i.e., meals) may be provided but is not essential. Establishments that manage short-stay accommodation establishments (e.g., hotels and motels) on a contractual basis are classified in this subsector if they both manage the operation and provide the operating staff. Such establishments are classified based on the type of facility managed and operated.

This industry also includes athletes and other celebrities exclusively engaged in endorsing products and making speeches or public appearances for which they receive a fee.

Category: 71: Arts, Entertainment, and Recreation/7113: Promoters of Performing Arts, Sports, and Similar Events
Name: Promoters and agents
Description:
BEA Code & Name is '711A00:Promoters of performing arts and sports and agents for public figures'. The Arts, Entertainment, and Recreation sector includes a wide range of establishments that operate facilities or provide services to meet varied cultural, entertainment, and recreational interests of their patrons. This sector comprises (1) establishments that are involved in producing, promoting, or participating in live performances, events, or exhibits intended for public viewing; (2) establishments that preserve and exhibit objects and sites of historical, cultural, or educational interest; and (3) establishments that operate facilities or provide services that enable patrons to participate in recreational activities or pursue amusement, hobby, and leisure-time interests.   Some establishments that provide cultural, entertainment, or recreational facilities and services are classified in other sectors. Excluded from this sector are: (1) establishments that provide both accommodations and recreational facilities, such as hunting and fishing camps and resort and casino hotels are classified in Subsector 721, Accommodation; (2) restaurants and night clubs that provide live entertainment in addition to the sale of food and beverages are classified in Subsector 722, Food Services and Drinking Places; (3) motion picture theaters, libraries and archives, and publishers of newspapers, magazines, books, periodicals, and computer software are classified in Sector 51, Information; and (4) establishments using transportation equipment to provide recreational and entertainment services, such as those operating sightseeing buses, dinner cruises, or helicopter rides, are classified in Subsector 487, Scenic and Sightseeing Transportation. Related NAICS codes are: 7113-4

Description:

The sports teams and clubs included in this industry may or may not operate their own arena, stadium, or other facility for presenting their games or other spectator sports events.

Description:
